impounded_tanker.scene: The Situation Room smells like stale coffee and carpet cleaner. You are three hours into your new assignment, and the wall of monitors already shows your first crisis: a grainy feed from Bandar Abbas harbor, where the MV Stellar Fortune sits under armed guard, her crew confined below decks. Admiral Chen taps the screen with a laser pointer. 'IRGC fast-boat swarm, sir. Six boats, coordinated approach, textbook harassment pattern.' Your predecessor's resignation letter is still warm in the shredder. The Joint Chiefs are sizing you up. Nobody in this room expects you to last the week.
consequences: You pick up the secure phone and dial the Iranian mission in Geneva. The room watches you work the diplomatic machinery — patient, methodical. Admiral Chen exchanges a look with his deputy. Iran's charge d'affaires listens politely, promises to 'convey your concerns.' The tanker sits in Bandar Abbas. The world sees restraint. Tehran sees something else entirely.

You say nothing. You simply nod at Admiral Chen. Within the hour, the USS Arleigh Burke alters course for Bandar Abbas, her radar arrays painting the Iranian coastline. No statement is issued. No calls are made. The image of an American destroyer loitering twelve nautical miles off an Iranian port speaks volumes. IRGC commanders begin making phone calls.

You reach for the phone, but not the one to Tehran. You call the Liberian maritime authority directly and propose a joint response — shared burden, shared optics. The flag-state ambassador is surprised, then intrigued. The coalition seed is planted before Iran can frame this as a bilateral confrontation.

characterFlavor.trump: You glance at the TV monitors lining the wall — every network is running the seizure footage on loop. This is the kind of crisis that makes or breaks a presidency on the six o'clock news.
characterFlavor.hegseth: You study the tactical display with a professional's eye — six fast-boats in a coordinated swarm pattern means someone planned this, trained for it, and is watching to see what you do next.
characterFlavor.kushner: Your phone buzzes — MBS, then MBZ, then Erdogan. Every Gulf leader wants reassurance, and reassurance is a currency you can spend.
characterFlavor.fuentes: Six thousand miles from American soil, an American ship sits under armed guard because fifty years of interventionist policy put it there.
characterFlavor.asmongold: Chat is already pulling satellite imagery and cross-referencing IRGC patrol schedules. Your crowd-sourced intel feed is three hours ahead of the CIA briefing.

predecessors_ghost.scene: The classified folder sits on the mahogany desk, sealed with red tape and a 'NOFORN' stamp. Your predecessor's chief of staff left it here deliberately — a parting gift or a poisoned chalice. The air conditioning hums. Through the reinforced windows, you can see the Rose Garden, impossibly green against the gray sky. Your hand hovers over the folder. The paper inside details secret negotiations with Tehran that never made the news. Your aide stands at the door, waiting. 'Sir, the morning brief starts in ten minutes.' The contents are radioactive either way — but so is ignorance.
consequences: You break the seal. The pages reveal a secret channel through Oman, three rounds of preliminary talks, and a document that maps Iran's actual red lines — not the ones they broadcast on state TV. Your predecessor was closer to a deal than anyone knew. The knowledge settles into your chest like a stone. You now understand Iran's real breaking point. Whether this saves you or destroys you depends on what you do next.

You pick up the folder, walk to the fireplace in the private study, and feed it to the flames. The paper curls, blackens, and disappears. Your chief of staff watches from the doorway. 'Clean start,' you tell him. He nods slowly. The public will trust a leader unburdened by backroom deals. But somewhere in Tehran, there is a red line you will now have to discover the hard way — through a crisis.

admirals_warning.scene: The Pentagon briefing room is windowless, lit by fluorescent panels that give everyone the complexion of the dead. Admiral Chen stands at the podium with a clicker in one hand and thirty years of naval warfare behind his eyes. The satellite imagery on the screen shows Iranian naval movements — Bandar Abbas, Jask, Qeshm Island — in sharp resolution. 'We need to increase patrol tempo immediately,' he says, his voice flat and certain. Behind you, your civilian advisors shift in their leather chairs. The Secretary of State clears her throat. The room divides along a fault line as old as the republic: military necessity versus diplomatic caution.
consequences: You nod. 'Do it, Admiral.' Chen closes his folder with quiet satisfaction. Three additional destroyer rotations are scheduled within the hour. Your civilian advisors file out in silence, resentment simmering beneath their professional composure. At the Pentagon, the word spreads: the new boss listens to the uniforms. In Tehran, satellite analysts note the increased tempo and begin their own calculations.

You raise a hand. 'I want my full team in the room before we change our posture.' Admiral Chen pauses, then nods — slowly, grudgingly. He respects the process, barely. Your national security team assembles for a two-hour session that produces a measured response. Everyone feels heard. The decision comes later, more informed but less decisive.

You lean forward. 'Admiral, what if we sent a different kind of signal?' The room goes quiet. You outline a diplomatic overture — direct message to Tehran through Swiss intermediaries. Chen's jaw tightens. He begins composing a memo to the Armed Services Committee chairman about your 'lack of seriousness.' But six thousand miles away, Iranian Foreign Minister Araghchi reads the signal and picks up his own phone.

journalists_call.scene: Your assistant appears in the doorway with that particular expression that means trouble. 'Emily Watkins, New York Times. She says it's urgent.' The name lands like a small grenade. Watkins broke the Abu Ghraib story. She exposed the NSA domestic surveillance program. She has a Pulitzer and the phone number of every disgruntled staffer in Washington. Now she wants to talk about your first seventy-two hours. 'Off the record,' she promises through the receiver. But you can hear her keyboard clicking in the background. The recorder is already running. It always is.
consequences: You take the call. For twenty minutes, you shape the narrative — measured, competent, aware of the stakes. Watkins asks probing questions but you stay ahead of her. The piece runs three days later: 'New Crisis Manager Brings Steady Hand to Strait Standoff.' But buried in paragraph sixteen is a detail you don't remember sharing. A 20% chance she burns you on the next one.

You dictate a written statement through your press secretary. Four paragraphs of careful nothing. Watkins runs her story anyway: 'New Strait Manager Stonewalls Press on First Day.' It's above the fold, framed by a photo that makes you look evasive. Safe, but the narrative is now hers to control.

You tell your assistant: 'No comment. And don't put her through again.' The line goes dead. Watkins sits in her Manhattan office, staring at her phone. She crosses your name off the cooperative list and adds it to another list entirely. Future media engagements just got significantly harder.

british_pm_call.scene: The call comes in on the encrypted line at 6:47 AM, before your first coffee has cooled. The British Prime Minister's voice carries the particular warmth of someone who wants something. He proposes a joint naval task force — shared command structure, shared rules of engagement, Royal Navy vessels integrated with the Fifth Fleet. It is generous. It is suspiciously generous. Through the Situation Room window, the first light catches the Washington Monument. Your national security advisor slides a note across the desk: 'UK wants our vote on the UN trade resolution. This is the price.' Nothing is free in the special relationship. It never has been.
consequences: You accept. The joint task force is announced within hours — a press conference at 10 Downing Street, a handshake between admirals. The coalition is real. But shared command means shared veto. You have just traded unilateral strike capability for the legitimacy of a partner. In Tehran, analysts note the development and begin calculating how to drive a wedge between London and Washington.

You counter-propose: parallel operations in the same area, separate command structures, shared intelligence. The PM goes quiet for a moment. 'Rather defeats the purpose, doesn't it?' he says, mildly. The UK is professionally insulted but too disciplined to show it. You keep your freedom of action. The coalition is thinner but real.

You lean back in your chair. 'What do you want in return?' The PM laughs — that particular English laugh that means you've said the quiet part out loud. He mentions the UN trade resolution. A side negotiation opens, and suddenly the strait crisis has become a chessboard with pieces you didn't know were in play.

first_seizure_attempt.scene: The FLASH traffic arrives at 0347 local, printed on thermal paper that curls at the edges. CENTCOM reports IRGC fast-boats converging on the MV Pacific Meridian — a Liberian-flagged tanker carrying Japanese electronics through the strait. Your watch officer's voice is tight: 'Six boats, closing at forty knots, weapons visible.' The tactical display shows the tanker as a slow green dot, surrounded by fast red ones. This is your first real test. The entire National Security Council is watching from their homes via secure video. You can see their faces in the blue light of their screens — waiting for you to decide.
first_seizure_attempt.urgency: CENTCOM is on the line. The fast-boats are seven minutes from boarding range. Decide now.
consequences: You authorize the scramble. Two F/A-18s roar off the Eisenhower's deck and bank hard toward the Pacific Meridian, their afterburners cutting the predawn darkness. The radio crackles with a verbal warning in Farsi and English. The IRGC boats hesitate — then two peel off. Then three more. The last boat lingers, its crew filming the American jets, before turning north. The seizure is deterred. Standard procedure, textbook execution. But you know: if this hadn't worked, you would own the failure.

You authorize warning shots. The bridge of the USS Porter erupts in controlled violence — .50 caliber rounds stitch the water two hundred meters ahead of the lead IRGC boat. The sound carries across the strait. On CNN, the footage plays in slow motion. The IRGC boats scatter. Ninety percent of the world exhales. But in Tehran, Tangsiri is already planning the next move, and the escalation is now real.

You let it happen. The words taste like ash in your mouth, but you say them: 'Document everything. Do not engage.' The Pacific Meridian is boarded. Her crew is taken below. Iranian flags replace the Liberian colors. It is painful to watch. But your UN ambassador is already preparing the footage for an emergency session, and the images of armed men boarding a commercial vessel will be devastating for Tehran's international standing.

characterFlavor.trump: The cable news chyrons are already writing themselves — 'TANKER SEIZED UNDER NEW LEADERSHIP' or 'DECISIVE ACTION IN THE STRAIT.' Which headline runs depends entirely on the next thirty seconds.
characterFlavor.hegseth: The tactical display shows a textbook intercept pattern — you've studied this exact formation in war college scenarios. The difference is, this time the dots on the screen are real boats with real weapons.
characterFlavor.kushner: You're already calculating the insurance premium implications — every tanker seized doubles the cost of strait transit, and every cost increase is leverage for the protection framework you've been building.
characterFlavor.fuentes: American taxpayers are funding a naval presence to protect foreign-flagged tankers carrying oil to countries that don't even like us. The base sees the absurdity.
characterFlavor.asmongold: Real-time seizure attempt unfolding on stream. Chat is counting the fast-boats, tracking the destroyer response time, and arguing about whether this confirms your 'escalation pattern' prediction from Day 2.

oil_markets_panic.scene: The Bloomberg terminal in the corner of the Situation Room flashes red numbers that climb like a fever. Oil futures jumped fifteen dollars a barrel overnight, and the trajectory shows no ceiling. Your Energy Secretary is on the phone, his voice strained: 'Gas prices are on every cable news chyron in America. Trucking companies are calling about fuel surcharges.' Through the window, you can see a CNN satellite truck setting up on the North Lawn. The Saudi Energy Minister has made it known, through back channels, that he might increase production — if you ask nicely. The word 'nicely' carries the weight of a thousand concessions.
consequences: You sign the order to release strategic petroleum reserves. The effect is immediate — futures drop eight dollars within the hour. Markets exhale. Gas station owners adjust their signs downward. But you watch the SPR counter tick down and feel the finite nature of the relief. You have bought yourself ten days, maybe twelve. The reserves are not a solution. They are borrowed time.

You swallow your pride and call Riyadh. MBS takes the call on the third ring, which means he was waiting. The conversation is cordial, professional, and deeply humiliating. He agrees to increase production by 500,000 barrels per day. The price will come later — it always does. On Day 20, the details of this conversation will leak, and the editorial cartoons will not be kind.

You do nothing. You tell your Energy Secretary to let the markets find their own level. The pain is immediate — gas stations raise prices, cable news runs 'CRISIS AT THE PUMP' graphics, and the opposition party's ad team begins cutting attack spots. But you know that artificial intervention only delays the reckoning. The markets will adjust. Your approval rating, however, is adjusting faster.

un_showdown.scene: The Security Council chamber is a theatre of controlled fury. Your ambassador sits in the American chair, earpiece in, waiting for your instructions relayed from six thousand miles away. Russia's ambassador is leaning back with studied indifference — but his aides are passing notes furiously. China's representative stares at the draft resolution, her pen hovering. The gallery is packed with journalists. The Iranian delegation sits stone-faced in the observer section. You have one shot at a binding resolution. One shot to make the international community put its name on paper. The clock on the wall reads 3:47 PM in New York, and the President of the Security Council is about to open the floor.
consequences: You push for the binding resolution with full sanctions. The vote proceeds in alphabetical order. Your heart hammers with each 'aye.' Russia — abstains. A murmur ripples through the chamber. China follows suit. The resolution passes. Iran is formally, legally, internationally isolated. Your ambassador permits herself the ghost of a smile.

You instruct your ambassador to support the non-binding statement of concern. It passes unanimously — fifteen hands raised in a gesture that means precisely nothing. In the hallway afterward, a European diplomat mutters, 'Well, that was symbolic.' Your team flies home demoralized. You had leverage, and you spent it on air.

Your ambassador stands and requests the floor. 'I would like to present classified intelligence to this body.' The chamber goes silent. Satellite photos appear on the screens — IRGC commanders identified by name, their operations mapped in granular detail. The Iranian delegation scrambles for their phones. The room is shocked. But in Langley, the Director of the CIA is watching with a clenched jaw. You just burned intelligence sources that took years to develop.

proxy_ignition.scene: The alert comes from CENTCOM's Red Sea desk: Houthi anti-ship missiles launched from the Yemeni coast, tracking toward a commercial vessel in the Bab el-Mandeb strait. The missile tracks appear on the tactical display as angry red arcs. The crisis, which you had carefully contained within the Strait of Hormuz, is metastasizing. Your budget officer slides a revised expenditure report across the table — the numbers are bleeding red. An aide whispers that the French ambassador has requested an urgent call. The map on the wall now shows two active theaters, and Iran's proxy network is waking up like a nervous system sensing pain.
consequences: You authorize the strike. Tomahawk missiles arc from the USS Gravely toward the Houthi launch site in western Yemen. The facility is destroyed — clean, proportional, professional. In Riyadh, the Saudi Defense Minister calls to express personal gratitude. But you have just opened a second front, and the budget line for 'kinetic operations' doubles overnight.

You choose defense over offense. Interceptor missiles destroy the Houthi weapons in flight, their fragments splashing harmlessly into the Red Sea. The tanker sails on, unharmed. Your admirals report the intercept as a success. But the Houthis still have their launchers, their supply chain, their will. They will try again in three days.

You pick up the phone to the Omani back-channel. The message to Tehran is simple and clear: proxy attacks will be treated as Iranian attacks. A red line, drawn in language that cannot be misunderstood. In Tehran, the message lands on two desks simultaneously — Araghchi's and Tangsiri's. If Araghchi believes you, the calculus changes. If Tangsiri doesn't, it changes nothing.

the_leak.scene: Your chief of staff walks in holding a tablet, his face the color of old cement. The Washington Post homepage fills the screen: 'EXCLUSIVE: Classified Strategy Memo Reveals Administration's Iran Playbook.' Every detail is there — the red lines, the escalation triggers, the fallback positions, even the code name for the back-channel. Someone in your inner circle has betrayed you. The secure conference room suddenly feels much larger and much emptier. Your senior advisors sit around the table, and one of them did this. You study their faces. The press pool is already gathering outside the West Wing.
consequences: You launch the investigation immediately. The FBI counterintelligence team arrives within hours, polygraphs in tow. Your staff is terrified — the innocent alongside the guilty. For three days, the investigation dominates internal operations, burning political capital with every interview. But if you find the leaker, and you make an example, your credibility will be ironclad. The mole hunt begins.

You lean back and smile. Your communications director catches the signal. Within an hour, the talking points are out: 'The leaked memo demonstrates the President's brilliant multi-track strategy.' It is audacious spin. Iran now knows your playbook, which is a genuine problem. But your base thinks you are playing four-dimensional chess, and the news cycle shifts from 'security breach' to 'strategic genius.'

You say nothing. No investigation, no spin, no comment. The news cycle churns for forty-eight hours and then moves on to the next outrage. But in the quiet corridors of your administration, a message has been received: there are no consequences for betrayal. The next leak will come faster.

congressional_hearing_big.scene: The hearing room in the Hart Senate Office Building is packed to the walls. Camera operators jostle for angles. The Senate Armed Services Committee chairman, a silver-haired veteran of three wars, peers down from the raised dais and opens with the line everyone will remember: 'The American people deserve to know why we're spending fifty million dollars a day on a crisis that's getting worse.' The C-SPAN cameras capture every bead of sweat, every shuffle of paper. Your legal counsel sits behind you, a stack of classified briefing cards ready. The gallery is full of reporters, lobbyists, and two families who lost sons in the strait.
consequences: You testify personally. For three hours, you face every question — hostile, sympathetic, and grandstanding alike. You came prepared, and it shows. The viral clip comes at the two-hour mark: you calmly dismantle the chairman's argument with a single declassified data point that makes him blink. 'Senator, with respect, your numbers are from 2019. Here are the current figures.' The room shifts. By evening, your approval ticks up three points.

You send your deputy. The official reason is 'ongoing crisis management demands the coordinator's presence.' The real reason is that you cannot afford to be pinned down on camera for four hours. Congress is insulted — deeply, professionally insulted. The chairman's closing statement includes the phrase 'contempt for this body's oversight role.' Future funding requests just became significantly harder.

You testify, and then you do something unprecedented: you declassify satellite imagery live on camera. The room gasps as the screen shows Iranian mine-laying operations in crystal clarity. The chairman's mouth opens, then closes. The Pentagon is furious about the unauthorized declassification. But the public — the public is with you, and the footage is on every screen in America by dinner.

characterFlavor.trump: The hearing room cameras are live on C-SPAN and every cable network. Three hours of free airtime — more than most candidates get in an entire primary season.
characterFlavor.hegseth: The senators want political theater; you brought operational data. Every chart, every satellite image, every casualty number is a fact that cuts through the grandstanding.
characterFlavor.kushner: You scan the committee members' faces, calculating which ones have donors connected to your Gulf contacts. Congressional hearings are about relationships, not testimony.
characterFlavor.fuentes: The chairman opens with spending numbers designed to make you look reckless. He voted for the Iraq War, the Libya intervention, and the Syria operation. Your base remembers, even if the cameras don't show it.
characterFlavor.asmongold: You're streaming the hearing prep live — chat is crowdsourcing rebuttals to the committee's likely questions. Viewer count just hit a new high. This is S-tier content.

iran_ultimatum.scene: Iranian state television fills the Situation Room's main screen. Mojtaba Khamenei stands at a podium flanked by IRGC flags, his father's portrait looming behind him. His voice is measured but his eyes are cold: 'Lift sanctions on our central bank within seventy-two hours, or the next vessel we seize will fly the American flag.' Behind him, Admiral Tangsiri stands with his arms crossed, the ghost of a smile on his lips. The room goes silent. Your watch officer stops writing. The clock on the wall becomes the loudest sound in the room. Seventy-two hours. Three days to decide whether this is a bluff you can call or a promise you cannot afford to test.
iran_ultimatum.urgency: The seventy-two-hour clock is already ticking. Tehran is watching your response in real time.
consequences: You walk to the podium in the White House press room. The cameras flash. Your voice is steady: 'America does not respond to threats. We will not be blackmailed. Period.' The room erupts with questions. In Tehran, the broadcast cuts to Tangsiri's face — he is smiling. If Iran follows through, you own the catastrophe. If they back down, you own the crisis. The next seventy-two hours will determine which.

You reach for the Omani back-channel. Your message is precise: mutual step-down, simultaneous gestures, no public humiliation for either side. Fifty-fifty odds, your intelligence people say. Araghchi is pushing for it internally — he sees the off-ramp. But Tangsiri wants blood, and the IRGC has its own chain of command. The counter-proposal travels through three intermediaries and lands on a desk in Tehran.

You do nothing visible. No statement, no press conference, no tweets. But beneath the surface, assets move: a carrier group repositions, allies are briefed on secure lines, submarine patrols intensify. Iran's intelligence apparatus watches and reports back conflicting assessments. Sometimes silence is the loudest response of all.

characterFlavor.trump: Mojtaba's face fills the screen, but you're already thinking about your response — which camera angle, which phrase will dominate the news cycle for the next seventy-two hours.
characterFlavor.hegseth: You assess the ultimatum through a military lens — is the IRGC positioned to execute, or is this political theater backed by insufficient force?
characterFlavor.kushner: An ultimatum is an opening bid dressed up as a final offer. You've seen this in a hundred real estate negotiations — the question is what they'll actually settle for.
characterFlavor.fuentes: Iran issued an ultimatum because American sanctions gave them nothing left to lose. The establishment's maximum pressure policy created the very crisis they're now asking you to solve with more military spending.
characterFlavor.asmongold: Seventy-two hour countdown timer on screen. Chat is already running probability models. This is the moment that proves whether your threat analysis was right or completely off.

intel_breakthrough.scene: The intelligence arrives at 2:14 AM — a convergence of signals intercepts and a human source in Tehran that changes everything. Your CIA briefer spreads the analysis across the conference table, her hands trembling slightly with the weight of what she is about to say. 'Sir, this isn't about the strait. It never was.' The intercepts reveal Iran's true strategic objective: forcing international recognition of Iranian regional hegemony. Every seizure, every provocation, every proxy attack — it was all building toward this moment of maximum leverage. The question is no longer what Iran is doing but what you do with the knowledge that you understand their endgame.
consequences: You share the intelligence with allies — a secure briefing for the Five Eyes, then NATO, then key Asian partners. The reaction is electric. Suddenly, disparate events click into a coherent picture. A coalition response begins to form, unified by shared understanding. But your intelligence methods are now exposed across a dozen foreign services. Future collection quality will degrade. You traded tomorrow's secrets for today's alliance.

You close the folder and lock it in your personal safe. Only three people know: you, the CIA briefer, and the DNI. The knowledge sits in your chest like a coal — hot, dangerous, powerful. You now see moves that no one else can see. But when the moment comes to act on it, your allies will feel blindsided, and blind allies make unpredictable partners.

You call Emily Watkins at the Times. 'I have something for you,' you say. 'On background.' The story runs within twelve hours, and suddenly the world sees what you see. Iran scrambles to change plans — the intelligence is partly obsolete now. But public pressure on Tehran surges, and the diplomatic landscape shifts. You used a secret as a weapon. It worked. This time.

characterFlavor.trump: The CIA briefer's trembling hands tell you this is big — the kind of intelligence revelation that plays perfectly in a declassified press conference with the right production value.
characterFlavor.hegseth: You absorb the intelligence assessment with a tactician's focus — if Iran's true objective is regional hegemony, then every force disposition you've planned needs recalibrating.
characterFlavor.kushner: Understanding Iran's real endgame means understanding their negotiating position. This isn't just intelligence — it's the foundation for a deal framework worth hundreds of millions.
characterFlavor.fuentes: The intelligence confirms what your base has been saying for years — Iran's ambitions are regional, not American. We're fighting someone else's war.
characterFlavor.asmongold: The briefer reveals Iran's true strategy, and it maps exactly to the pattern you identified on stream last week. If chat clipped that analysis, your credibility metric is about to explode.

journalist_returns.scene: Emily Watkins is back, and this time she is not asking for quotes. She wants to be embedded with your team for forty-eight hours — 'radical transparency,' she calls it, leaning forward in the visitor's chair with the kind of intensity that wins Pulitzers. Your press secretary stands behind her, making throat-cutting gestures. But Watkins has been fair so far, and the public narrative needs shaping. Through the window, you can see the cable news trucks multiplying on the North Lawn. The story is being written with or without your cooperation. The only question is whether you hold the pen.
consequences: You give her the full on-the-record interview. Ninety minutes in your office, no ground rules, no pre-screened questions. Your narrative is coherent because your strategy is coherent, and it shows. The piece runs as a Sunday magazine cover: 'The Steady Hand.' For the first time in weeks, the press is on your side.

You let her in. For forty-eight hours, Watkins shadows your team through briefings, calls, and the small human moments between crises. She sees the mistakes — the 3 AM wrong call, the briefing card you read upside down. But she also sees the competence, the dedication, the human cost. There is a seventy percent chance the resulting profile is glowing. There is a thirty percent chance it ends your career.

You feed her a specific story — a carefully constructed narrative about the back-channel progress, designed to pressure Iran through public opinion. Watkins runs it exactly as you intended. It works beautifully, this time. But journalists have institutional memory about being used as instruments of policy, and Emily Watkins has the longest memory in Washington.

carrier_incident.scene: The footage arrives from the USS Eisenhower's gun cameras: an Iranian Shahed drone, angular and menacing, buzzing the carrier's flight deck at fifty feet. You can see the deck crew hitting the steel, hands over their heads, as the drone's shadow passes over them. The bridge recording captures the officer of the deck shouting 'Hostile UAV, hostile UAV!' before the drone banks away and disappears into the haze. CENTCOM wants to release the footage immediately. Iran's state media is already claiming it was 'routine surveillance of international waters.' Your communications team and your military advisors are, for once, in perfect agreement: someone needs to pay for this.
consequences: You issue a formal protest through military-to-military channels. A stern letter, delivered through the deconfliction line, citing international law and demanding cessation. Professional. Measured. By the book. In Tehran, the protest is read, filed, and forgotten. Within the IRGC, the consensus hardens: the Americans flinched. The next provocation will be closer.

You declassify the footage and hand it to CNN. By evening, the gun-camera video is on every screen in the world. The image of American sailors diving for cover on their own flight deck is indelible. Iran is globally embarrassed. Condemnations pour in from thirty nations. But Admiral Tangsiri watches the footage in his office, and the humiliation burns. He will remember this.

You authorize the strike. A single Tomahawk cruise missile, precisely targeted, destroys the drone command facility on the Iranian coast. The explosion is visible from the Eisenhower's deck. The world holds its breath for forty-eight hours. Iran goes silent — no state media statements, no UN complaints, nothing. The silence is either retreat or planning.

secret_talks.scene: The message arrives via the Omani ambassador, handwritten on embassy stationery in Farsi and English. Iranian moderates — the Araghchi faction, your analysts believe — are proposing secret talks. Muscat, neutral ground, no staff beyond two per side. The terms are simple: show up and talk. But the intelligence assessment attached to the message carries a warning in red ink: if these talks leak, hawks on both sides will use the exposure to destroy the diplomatic track permanently. Your chief of staff reads the message over your shoulder. 'This could be the breakthrough,' he says. Then, quieter: 'Or the trap.'
consequences: You send your envoy to Muscat. The talks begin in a whitewashed villa overlooking the Gulf of Oman. Both sides agree to a cooling-off period — reduced naval patrols, no new seizures, a forty-eight-hour window of calm. It is fragile, unofficial, and deniable. But for the first time, someone on the other side looked you in the eye and said 'enough.'

You reject the offer. The risk calculus does not favor secret diplomacy — too many variables, too many leakers, too many ways this becomes your scandal. The Omani ambassador nods sadly and relays your response. In Tehran, the moderates lose their argument. Iranian hardliners use the rejection as proof that America was never serious about peace.

You leak the offer to a friendly reporter. The headline reads: 'Iran Begs for Secret Talks as Strait Crisis Deepens.' You gain the public credit for being the reasonable party. But the diplomatic bridge is burned — the Omani ambassador calls personally to express his 'profound disappointment.' The back-channel goes dark.

congress_pressure.scene: The Senate Armed Services Committee hearing room is half-empty but fully hostile. The chairman adjusts his microphone with the practiced slowness of a man who knows the cameras are rolling. 'We have serious concerns about the trajectory of spending,' he begins, shuffling a stack of budget projections. Your budget liaison whispers the number: they are threatening to cut your operational funding by thirty percent. The committee staffers have prepared a damning chart that compares your daily expenditure to the cost of school lunches. It is reductive and effective.
consequences: You open the classified folder. Satellite imagery, intercepted communications, the IRGC order of battle — all of it laid before the committee in a secure session. The senators lean forward. The chairman's hostility softens into something approaching concern. 'Why weren't we briefed sooner?' he asks. Congress is satisfied, for now. But the intelligence community notices that their crown jewels were used to win a budget fight.

You promise to cut spending. The words taste like compromise, but you deliver them with conviction. 'We will reduce daily operational costs by twenty percent within two weeks.' Congress relents, the budget survives, and your staff begins the grim work of deciding which patrols to cancel and which intelligence assets to mothball.

You stonewall the committee. 'The operational details of this mission are classified for the safety of our personnel.' The chairman's face hardens. Three senators make statements to the press afterward, each more hostile than the last. By evening, the media narrative has shifted from the strait to your 'arrogance and contempt for oversight.'

allied_request.scene: The Japanese ambassador arrives at the State Department with a formal request printed on cream-colored paper bearing the chrysanthemum seal. Japan — the world's fourth-largest economy, importer of 88% of its oil through the Strait of Hormuz — is requesting dedicated US Navy escorts for its tanker fleet. The ambassador bows slightly as he presents the document. Behind the diplomatic courtesy, there is real fear. Tokyo's strategic petroleum reserve will last sixty days. After that, the lights begin to go out in the world's most energy-dependent nation.
consequences: You assign the escorts. Within forty-eight hours, Japanese-flagged tankers sail through the strait under the watchful guns of US Navy destroyers. In Tokyo, the Prime Minister makes a public statement of gratitude that leads every Japanese news broadcast. The alliance is strengthened. Your naval commanders wince at the stretched resources but comply without complaint.

You decline, politely and with genuine regret. The ambassador absorbs the news with characteristic composure, but you can see the disappointment in the slight tightening around his eyes. Japan begins quietly exploring alternative supply routes and emergency rationing plans. The alliance endures, but a small crack has appeared in its foundation.

humanitarian.scene: The report comes from your destroyer captain, USS Halsey, patrolling the eastern approach to the strait: an Iranian fishing vessel has capsized in heavy swells three nautical miles south of Qeshm Island. Forty fishermen are in the water. The captain's voice is calm but urgent on the radio — he can see them through his binoculars, heads bobbing in the gray-green waves. Your destroyer is fifteen minutes away at flank speed. Iranian coast guard vessels are forty-five minutes out. The water temperature is survivable for about an hour. The fishermen are not soldiers. They are fathers, brothers, sons. But this is an active naval confrontation zone, and your ship pulling alongside an Iranian vessel creates risks that no rule of engagement fully addresses.
consequences: You give the order: rescue immediately. The USS Halsey comes about hard, her wake curving white against the dark water. Fifteen minutes later, American sailors are pulling Iranian fishermen from the sea — thirty-eight survivors hauled over the gunwales, wrapped in blankets, given water. A petty officer holds an old man's hand while a corpsman checks his vitals. The footage, captured by the ship's cameras, goes viral within hours. For one moment, in the midst of a geopolitical crisis, humanity prevails.

You call the Iranian naval liaison through the deconfliction channel. A joint rescue is coordinated — the USS Halsey provides on-scene coordination while Iranian coast guard vessels handle the recovery. Most of the crew is saved. The cooperation is awkward, tense, and ultimately successful. No one calls it a breakthrough, but somewhere in the machinery of both navies, a small thread of professional respect forms.

You order the Halsey to maintain its patrol route. The mission comes first. The fishermen are in Iranian waters; Iran can handle its own. Twelve hours later, Iranian state media broadcasts footage of empty life jackets floating in the strait, with the caption: 'American warships watched as Iranian fishermen drowned.' The footage is devastating. The truth — that your ship was on an active defense patrol — does not matter. The image does.

media_crisis.scene: The footage is ninety seconds of pure chaos: an IRGC fast-boat cutting across the bow of the USS Bataan at what appears to be twenty meters, the American ship sounding its horn, a near-collision that sends spray across both vessels. Someone on the Bataan's bridge screams an obscenity that the microphone catches perfectly. Cable news is running it on a twenty-four-hour loop with the chyron 'NEAR MISS IN THE STRAIT.' The full unedited video, which you have seen, tells a different story — the Iranian boat initiated the provocation by dead-heading toward the Bataan at high speed. But the public only sees the version that sells.
consequences: You release the full, unedited video — all twelve minutes, including the Iranian boat's aggressive approach from two kilometers out. The narrative shifts within hours. Military analysts on CNN point to the Iranian vessel's intercept course. 'This was a deliberate provocation,' says a retired admiral on camera. Your version of events prevails. Iran's state media goes quiet on the subject.

You downplay the incident. Your press secretary calls it 'a routine encounter that has been sensationalized.' No video release, no finger-pointing. The story fades from the news cycle within forty-eight hours. Both sides quietly move on, and the operational tempo in the strait returns to its tense equilibrium.

You go on the offensive. A formal public condemnation, backed by the video evidence, names the IRGC boat commander and demands Iran discipline its forces. Your base rallies. Cable news runs your statement alongside the footage. But in Tehran, the condemnation is played on state TV as evidence of American aggression, and the cycle of provocation accelerates.

intel_reveal.scene: The intelligence analyst stands at the Situation Room podium, her hands steady despite what she is about to reveal. 'We have located Iran's primary mine-laying operation,' she says, clicking to a satellite image so sharp you can read the hull numbers. A warehouse complex near Bandar Abbas, three vessels loaded with mines, and a timeline that suggests deployment within seventy-two hours. The intelligence comes from two sources: a signals intercept and a human asset who has been cultivated for four years. Acting on this intelligence will neutralize the mine threat. It will also burn the source — the patterns of response will tell Iranian counterintelligence exactly where the leak is.
consequences: You authorize the strike. Eight cruise missiles reduce the depot to smoking rubble. Secondary explosions confirm the mines were real. The mine threat is neutralized overnight. But in Tehran, IRGC counterintelligence begins working backward from the strike pattern, and your human source has approximately forty-eight hours before the knock comes at his door.

You share the intelligence with coalition partners through secure channels. Allied minesweeping vessels deploy to the areas identified by your source, clearing sea lanes under the cover story of 'routine mine countermeasures.' The operation is slower but effective. Your source remains safe, and the coalition deepens its trust in American intelligence sharing.

You file the intelligence and do nothing. The analysis goes into a secure vault, tagged for future reference. Your source remains safe, the mine depot remains operational, and the knowledge sits like a loaded weapon — dangerous until you decide to pull the trigger, but useless until then.

china_mediation.scene: The Chinese ambassador requests a private meeting — no aides, no notes, no record. He arrives at the State Department through a side entrance, carrying nothing but a single sheet of paper. The proposal is simple: Beijing will pressure Iran to stand down in the strait in exchange for eased technology restrictions on three Chinese semiconductor firms. The ambassador slides the paper across the table. 'Minister Wang believes this crisis offers a unique opportunity for our nations to demonstrate cooperative leadership,' he says, his English impeccable. The offer is genuine. The price is steep. And the geopolitical implications extend far beyond the strait.
consequences: You accept the deal. Beijing delivers within the week — Iranian fast-boat patrols decrease, a seized tanker is released, and IRGC rhetoric softens noticeably. In Washington, the tech concessions draw sharp criticism from both parties. 'Trading semiconductor sovereignty for temporary peace,' a senator thunders. But the strait is quieter than it has been in weeks.

You counter-propose: limited technology exchanges in non-strategic sectors, with quarterly reviews tied to Iranian behavior. The Chinese ambassador listens, nods, and relays the counter to Beijing. Negotiations drag on for two weeks, producing modest gains and a diplomatic relationship that is marginally warmer. The crisis continues, but a channel is open.

You reject the proposal outright. 'China's technology ambitions and Iran's aggression are separate matters,' you tell the ambassador. Hawks in Congress applaud your firmness. The Chinese ambassador gathers his paper and leaves without a handshake. Within a month, Chinese arms shipments to Iran increase. The diplomatic window narrows.

houthi_attack.scene: The CENTCOM alert scrolls across every screen in the Situation Room: Houthi anti-ship missile launch detected from the Yemeni coast, tracking toward a Panamanian-flagged bulk carrier in the southern Red Sea. The missile is an Iranian-supplied C-802 derivative — you recognize it from the intelligence briefings. The carrier's crew of twenty-six has no idea what is heading their way at Mach 0.9. Your Red Sea desk officer is already on the phone with the Fifth Fleet. Iran's proxy war has expanded beyond the Gulf, and every day it remains unanswered, it grows bolder.
consequences: Precision strikes light up the Yemeni coast. F/A-18s from the Eisenhower drop JDAMs on the launch site, the mobile radar unit, and two suspected ammunition storage facilities. The Houthi launch capability is degraded — for now. But the Houthis have survived worse and rebuilt faster, and the footage of explosions in Yemen will play very differently in the Arab world than in Washington.

You ask Saudi Arabia to handle it. The call to Riyadh is carefully framed: 'This is your neighborhood.' The Saudi military engages with a mix of airstrikes and ground-based interdiction. Results are mixed — the Houthis are experienced guerrilla fighters who learned to disperse after years of Saudi bombardment. But the burden is shared, and the optics of an Arab-led response are better than an American one.

You condemn the attack in a formal statement and do nothing more. The press secretary reads the words with appropriate gravity. Critics immediately ask why the world's most powerful navy is watching commercial shipping get attacked. The question echoes through cable news for days, unanswered.

militia_attack.scene: The rockets hit at 0317 local time — three 107mm Katyushas, launched from a pickup truck parked in a palm grove three kilometers from the base perimeter. Two impact inside the wire. Your base operations center calls it in: two soldiers wounded, one critically, shrapnel from a rocket that hit the chow hall. The video from the base security cameras shows the flash, the dust cloud, the soldiers running for cover in their underwear. CENTCOM's targeting cell has already identified the militia group — Kata'ib Hezbollah, Iranian-funded, Iranian-trained. The strike authorization request is on your desk. The blood on the chow hall floor is still wet.
consequences: You authorize the retaliatory strike. Four hours after the attack, precision munitions destroy the militia's weapons warehouse and a command-and-control node. The message is clear and immediate: attack American forces, lose your infrastructure. In Baghdad, the militia's leadership goes to ground. The wounded soldier is medevacked to Landstuhl. The other soldier will carry shrapnel in his shoulder for the rest of his life.

You order increased base security — additional patrols, counter-rocket systems, hardened shelters. You absorb the attack without retaliating. Some in the press call it restraint. Others call it weakness. The militia fires again six days later, from a different position. The soldiers on base begin sleeping in their body armor.

You order the withdrawal from the base. The logistics are completed in seventy-two hours — efficient, professional, and devastating to morale. Iranian state media runs the footage of empty hangars and lowered flags as a victory montage. 'The Americans flee,' the anchor says, smiling. Your troops watch the broadcast from their new position, sixty kilometers further from the fight.

assassination_intel.scene: The briefing is delivered by a CIA officer who does not give her name. She places a red-bordered folder on the table and opens it to reveal a surveillance photograph: a man in civilian clothes, photographed through a car window in Istanbul. 'We have detected a credible assassination threat against senior officials involved in the strait crisis,' she says. 'The operational pattern is consistent with IRGC Quds Force, but we cannot rule out domestic extremists.' She pauses. 'The threat level is imminent.' The room temperature seems to drop. Your Secret Service detail, who were briefed thirty minutes ago, have already tripled the protection perimeter.
consequences: Security protocols go to maximum. Your movements are restricted, public appearances cancelled, the motorcade route randomized. The government operates from hardened facilities for seventy-two hours. Operations slow to a crawl as every meeting requires additional security screening. The threat is contained, but the image of a government operating from bunkers is not one that inspires confidence.

You go public. A carefully worded statement acknowledges the threat without naming the source. 'We will not be intimidated,' you say from the podium. The public rallies in support. But in Tehran and in domestic extremist chat rooms, the announcement is read as confirmation that their operations are having an effect. Tensions ratchet higher.

You ignore the threat and maintain your normal schedule. The Secret Service protests formally. You appear at a public event the next morning, shaking hands and projecting calm. The risk is real — the intelligence is specific enough to include a timeline. But showing fear is a concession you refuse to make. Your security detail watches every rooftop.

domestic_unrest.scene: The aerial footage fills the Situation Room screens: protests in Washington, Chicago, Los Angeles, Houston. Tens of thousands in the streets, carrying signs that read 'NO WAR FOR OIL' and 'BRING THEM HOME.' A gas station in Detroit was set on fire overnight. The National Guard commander for the District of Columbia has submitted a formal request for authorization to deploy. Your domestic policy advisor sits across from you, her face drawn. 'The polling shows sixty-two percent oppose escalation,' she says. 'And thirty-eight percent believe the crisis is being mismanaged.' On the screen, a protester in a wheelchair — a veteran — is being interviewed by CNN. He is articulate, sympathetic, and devastating.
consequences: You address the nation from the Oval Office. The speech is seventeen minutes long, and you write the final paragraph yourself. You speak of shared sacrifice, of the importance of the strait to every American family, of the path toward resolution. You do not promise it will be easy. The polls stabilize — not a surge, but the bleeding stops. The veteran in the wheelchair watches from his living room and nods, once.

You deploy the National Guard. The troops take up positions around federal buildings, their presence meant to deter violence. Instead, the images of uniformed soldiers facing American citizens make headlines around the world. 'Is this Tehran or DC?' a European newspaper asks. The protests grow larger. The crackdown has become the story.

You announce a peace initiative — a bold, public pivot toward diplomatic resolution. Moderates across the spectrum applaud. Peace organizations endorse it. But hawks in your own party and in the military establishment view it as capitulation under domestic pressure. The announcement satisfies neither the streets nor the war room.

hostage.scene: Twelve faces stare from the split screen in the Situation Room — passport photos of the crew seized from the tanker MV Coral Dawn, now held in an unknown location in Iran. A Filipino engineer. A Greek captain. An Indian second officer. Their families have been on CNN for three straight days. A mother from Mumbai breaks down on camera, her words translated in real time. Your hostage negotiation team has identified a potential channel through Swiss intermediaries, but Tehran is not responding. The IRGC is using the crew as leverage, and every hour they remain in captivity, the pressure on you intensifies.
consequences: You open the channel through Bern. The Swiss ambassador in Tehran makes discreet contact. The negotiations begin slowly — Iran wants sanctions relief, you want proof of life. After seventy-two hours of back-and-forth, a video arrives: twelve crew members, alive, reading from prepared statements. The channel is open. The crew is scared but breathing.

You demand unconditional release in a public statement, backed by a new round of targeted sanctions against IRGC Navy commanders. International opinion turns sharply against Iran — the UN Secretary General issues a personal statement calling for the crew's release. The pressure builds, but the crew remains in Iranian hands, and pressure does not equal progress.

You authorize a quiet concession: a minor sanctions waiver on Iranian medical supplies, routed through an intermediary so the connection is deniable. Three days later, the crew is released at the Omani border, gaunt but alive. They fly home to tearful reunions broadcast worldwide. Nobody talks about the price. Nobody needs to.

oil_spike.scene: The trading floor at NYMEX looks like a hospital during a code blue. Oil futures have spiked fifteen dollars a barrel in overnight trading, and the trajectory is vertical. Your Energy Secretary's voice on the phone carries the particular strain of a man who has been fielding calls from refinery executives since 4 AM. 'We need to signal stability,' he says. 'Or this becomes a panic.' The gas price ticker on your desk — a daily briefing item now — reads $5.47 and climbing. Trucking companies are calling the Transportation Department about emergency fuel allocations. The markets are not just nervous; they are beginning to price in the possibility that this crisis has no resolution.
consequences: You authorize the SPR release. The announcement goes out at 9:00 AM Eastern, timed for maximum market impact. Futures drop immediately — six dollars in the first hour. The physical drawdown begins at Bryan Mound and Big Hill. Markets stabilize. Gas station signs tick downward. But the Strategic Petroleum Reserve counter on your desk shows a number that gets smaller every day.

You step to the podium for a verbal intervention. 'Markets should have confidence in the stability of global oil supply,' you say, your voice calibrated for maximum steadiness. It helps — a little. Futures drop three dollars on the statement. But traders are professional skeptics, and words without barrels behind them have a short half-life. Markets remain jittery through the week.

You let the markets correct on their own. No intervention, no statement, no signal. Gas prices soar past six dollars in some markets. Cable news discovers that your Energy Secretary drives an electric vehicle. Critics call you out of touch. The economic pain is real and politically devastating, but you hold to the principle that government intervention distorts markets and delays adjustment.

gulf_offer.scene: The offer arrives through the Saudi ambassador, delivered over coffee at a private meeting that appears on no official schedule. Saudi Arabia and the UAE will fund sixty percent of your strait operations — patrol costs, intelligence sharing, logistics support. The number is staggering: hundreds of millions of dollars flowing from Gulf sovereign wealth funds to offset your hemorrhaging budget. The catch arrives in the second paragraph: they want a seat at the rules-of-engagement table. Not a veto, exactly. But 'meaningful consultation on kinetic decisions.' Your budget officer, who has been running deficit projections that keep him up at night, looks at you with something approaching hope.
consequences: You accept with conditions — their money, your operational control. A carefully drafted memorandum of understanding gives Gulf partners consultation rights but preserves American command authority over all kinetic decisions. The money flows. Your budget stabilizes. Gulf partners feel invested, literally, in the outcome. The arrangement works because both sides need it to.

You accept fully. Gulf funding pours in, generous and immediate. Your budget crisis evaporates overnight. But in the first week, the Saudi military liaison asks to review a strike package before authorization, and you realize that 'consultation' has different meanings in Riyadh than in Washington. Critics ask who is really calling the shots in the strait.

You decline. The Saudi ambassador absorbs the rejection with the practiced grace of a man accustomed to hearing 'no' from Americans. You maintain complete independence. Your budget continues to bleed. The opportunity for burden-sharing passes, and with it a chance to deepen Gulf partnerships that might outlast this crisis.

drone_shootdown.scene: The surveillance feed from the MQ-9 Reaper cuts to static at 0623 local time. Your drone, operating over international waters in the Gulf of Oman, has been destroyed by an Iranian surface-to-air missile. The telemetry data, recovered before the link was severed, shows the SA-15 battery that fired. The Joint Chiefs are in your office within the hour, the targeting package already prepared. 'Proportional response, sir,' the Chairman says, laying photos of the Iranian missile battery on your desk. 'We take out the battery that shot down our drone. Clean, professional, defensible.' The wreckage of your forty-million-dollar aircraft is sinking to the bottom of the Gulf of Oman.
consequences: You authorize the strike. A single Tomahawk destroys the SAM site with clinical precision. The explosion is captured by another drone — you always have another drone — and the footage is released to confirm the proportionality. The world holds its breath. Iran issues a furious condemnation but does not retaliate. The message has been sent, received, and understood.

You flood the zone. Within twenty-four hours, the number of surveillance drones over the Gulf doubles. Where there was one MQ-9, now there are four. Iran watches them circle overhead and does not fire again. The implicit message is elegant: shoot one down, and we send more. The drone swarm becomes its own deterrent.

You stand down. 'It was unmanned,' you tell the Joint Chiefs. 'No American blood was spilled. We absorb this.' The Chairman nods, his expression unreadable. Some in the press call it wisdom — proportional responses invite proportional counter-responses, and nobody dies over a drone. Others call it weakness. In Tehran, the calculus shifts: if we can shoot down their drones without consequence, what else can we do?

un_vote.scene: The Security Council chamber in New York is quiet with the particular tension of a vote that matters. The draft resolution condemning Iran's actions in the strait is on the table. Your ambassador has spent three days negotiating language — 'condemns' versus 'deplores,' 'demands' versus 'calls upon.' Russia's ambassador has made it clear, through intermediaries, that a veto awaits any resolution with enforcement teeth. China is performing its customary ambiguity, signaling different things to different delegations. The vote is sixty seconds away.
consequences: You push the strong resolution. Russia vetoes, as promised — the ambassador's hand raised with theatrical regret. China follows. The resolution fails. But the debate itself — broadcast live to the world — has isolated Iran diplomatically. The transcript of the Iranian ambassador's defensive, stammering response plays on loop. Sometimes losing the vote wins the argument.

You accept the watered-down version. 'Deplores' instead of 'condemns.' 'Calls upon' instead of 'demands.' It passes unanimously — fifteen hands raised in a gesture of international consensus. The resolution is symbolic, toothless, and unanimous. Your team spins it as unity. The Iranian ambassador shrugs.

You withdraw the resolution entirely. Your ambassador explains that 'we seek a stronger framework than the current political landscape permits.' Allies are confused by the retreat. Russia is surprised. Iran is suspicious. The tactical withdrawal leaves everyone uncertain about your next move, which may be the point — or may be nothing at all.

cyber_attack_decision.scene: The NSA director arrives with a single USB drive in a sealed pouch. The briefing is for your eyes only — no aides, no notes, no recording. 'We have penetrated Iran's naval command-and-control network,' she says, her voice flat. 'On your authorization, we can disable their entire fleet coordination system for forty-eight hours. Their boats will be blind, deaf, and unable to communicate.' She pauses. 'If this is attributed to us, it constitutes an act of war under most international frameworks. If it's not attributed, it's the single most effective non-kinetic action we can take.' The USB drive sits on the table between you like an unexploded ordnance.
consequences: You approve the operation. The attack goes live at 0200 local time. Within the hour, Iran's naval command frequencies go silent. Fast-boats return to port, unable to coordinate. For forty-eight hours, the strait is the quietest it has been since the crisis began. Iran suspects sabotage — they know, but they cannot prove it. The deniability holds. For now.

You deny the operation. The USB drive goes back in its sealed pouch. 'Too dangerous,' you tell the NSA director. 'Save that card for when we really need it.' She nods — she expected this answer. The cyber weapon sits in reserve, growing more valuable with each day you do not use it. The question is whether the day you really need it comes before the vulnerability is patched.

election_pressure.scene: Your political advisors have taken over the Roosevelt Room, their laptops open to polling data that tells a grim story. 'Down six points in the suburbs,' your campaign manager says. 'Down nine among independents.' A dramatic move before the midterms could reverse the trend — or accelerate it. The television in the corner shows a cable news panel debating whether the strait crisis helps or hurts you politically. Your pollster slides a memo across the table: 'Voters respond to strength, not nuance.' The advisors want action. The question is whether you govern for the polls or govern for the policy.
consequences: You stage a naval exercise — Operation Iron Shield. Two carrier groups, forty ships, visible from space. The footage is spectacular: jets launching, missiles firing, sailors standing at attention on flight decks. Polls bump three points overnight. Iran calls it 'dangerous provocation.' Your campaign team calls it 'presidential leadership.' The truth, as always, lives somewhere in between.

You focus on diplomacy. A quiet round of talks, a measured statement about de-escalation. The pundits debate whether it is leadership or weakness. Your poll numbers do not move. Your advisors are frustrated. But the diplomatic track advances by one small, invisible step that will not appear in any headline or any poll.

You ignore the advisors. 'The crisis does not care about the election,' you tell them. 'And the election does not care about the crisis.' The polls are what they are. You govern based on the intelligence, the military assessment, and the diplomatic landscape. Your political team walks out deflated. History may vindicate you. The electorate may not.

pipeline_sabotage.scene: The underwater pipeline feeding the Fujairah terminal has ruptured. Video from an ROV shows a clean cut in the steel — too precise for corrosion, too symmetrical for an anchor strike. Your intelligence analysts are divided: the Iranian intelligence service has the capability, but so do half a dozen non-state actors. The oil terminal's throughput has dropped forty percent. Your forensics team needs seventy-two hours to analyze the cut pattern, the explosive residue, and the underwater current data. In the meantime, oil markets have already rendered their verdict: futures up eight dollars on the word 'sabotage.'
consequences: You blame Iran publicly. The accusation is delivered from the State Department podium, backed by circumstantial evidence and intelligence assessments. Allies rally. Public opinion shifts. But in the intelligence community, analysts who are not yet certain exchange uncomfortable glances. If the forensics tell a different story, the accusation will become your credibility's grave.

You investigate first. A methodical, public, transparent investigation — forensic divers, international observers, a timeline for findings. The markets hate the uncertainty. Cable news fills the vacuum with speculation. But the investigation will eventually produce facts, and facts are the one currency that does not depreciate.

You propose a joint investigation with Iran. The offer is unprecedented — a gesture of either radical trust or radical strategy. To everyone's surprise, Iran agrees. A team of American and Iranian engineers descends to the pipeline together. In the cold water, far from politics, professionals examine the evidence. A thread of trust forms in the most unlikely of places.

russia_arms_deal.scene: The satellite imagery is unambiguous: a Russian cargo vessel, the MV Akademik Chernov, transiting the Caspian Sea toward the Iranian port of Bandar-e Anzali. Her deck cargo, partially covered by tarps, shows the distinctive silhouette of Kh-35 anti-ship missile launchers. Your intelligence assessment estimates forty missiles in the shipment — enough to make the Strait of Hormuz significantly deadlier. The Russian ambassador has not returned your calls. The shipment will arrive in Iranian hands within seventy-two hours unless you act.
consequences: You intercept the shipment. A US Navy vessel hails the Akademik Chernov in international waters and orders it to heave to for inspection. Russia is furious — their ambassador calls it 'state piracy.' But the missiles do not reach Iran, and the diplomatic fallout, while severe, is manageable. Moscow files a protest at the UN. Tehran gets nothing.

You issue a formal diplomatic protest. A strongly worded demarche is delivered to the Russian Foreign Ministry. The protest is noted, filed, and the shipment arrives on schedule. World opinion shifts slightly — Russia's role as arms supplier is now documented. But the missiles are deployed on the Iranian coast within two weeks.

You do nothing. The missiles are unloaded at Bandar-e Anzali, transported to coastal batteries, and operational within a month. Iranian anti-ship capability increases measurably. Your Navy adjusts its patrol patterns to account for the new threat geometry. The strait has become more dangerous, and the cost of inaction compounds daily.

iran_internal.scene: The CIA station chief in the region delivers the briefing personally, a rare signal of its importance. 'The moderate faction within Iran's government is challenging the IRGC hardliners,' she says, spreading intercepted communications across the table. 'Foreign Minister Araghchi has been making private arguments for de-escalation. He's gaining traction with the civilian government. But Tangsiri and the IRGC leadership are pushing back hard.' She looks at you directly. 'You have a narrow window to tip the balance. But any overt move could backfire — the moderates are already accused of being Western stooges.'
consequences: You authorize covert support — quiet channels, deniable funding, strategic intelligence sharing through third parties. The support shifts the internal debate incrementally. Araghchi's faction gains ground in the Supreme National Security Council. IRGC funding requests are questioned for the first time. The change is invisible from the outside, but inside Tehran's power structure, the plates are shifting.

You publicly endorse the moderate faction. Your speech, carried on BBC Persian, is eloquent and sincere. But in Tehran, the effect is catastrophic. The moderates are immediately labeled American puppets. IRGC propaganda organs run your speech alongside footage of past American interventions. Araghchi's position becomes untenable within days.

You stay out of it entirely. Iranian politics will play out on Iranian terms, in Iranian time. Your analysts watch and report, but you issue no orders. The internal struggle continues without external interference. The outcome is uncertain, but whatever emerges will be genuinely Iranian — and therefore more durable than anything you could engineer.

tanker_escort.scene: The request comes not from one shipping company but from twelve — a joint letter signed by Maersk, Evergreen, COSCO, and nine others, delivered to the State Department and the Pentagon simultaneously. They want military escort through the strait. Without it, they say, commercial transit will cease within two weeks. The insurance companies are already pulling coverage. Your naval commander in the Gulf has run the numbers: full escort operations would require every available destroyer, plus carrier air cover, plus mine countermeasures vessels. It is possible, but barely, and it would stretch your forces to the breaking point.
consequences: You launch full escort operations. Convoys form up in the Gulf of Oman and transit the strait under the watchful presence of American warships, their radars sweeping, their weapons armed. Oil markets stabilize within days. The shipping companies resume transit. It works — at the cost of operational flexibility and crew fatigue that will compound over weeks.

You offer limited escort for allied-flagged vessels only. American, British, Japanese, and Australian tankers sail under protection. Ships flying flags of convenience — Liberia, Panama, Marshall Islands — are on their own. The policy is defensible but creates a two-tier system. Some tankers transit safely. Others reroute. The strait becomes a divided highway.

You decline the request. The military assessment is clear: escort operations in a contested strait are an invitation to escalation. The shipping companies reroute around the Cape of Good Hope. Transit times double. Costs triple. Gas prices surge. Your inbox fills with letters from Congressional offices, each one angrier than the last.

wargame_leak.scene: The headline lands like a bomb: 'Pentagon Wargame Projects 2,000 US Casualties in Strait Conflict.' Someone leaked a classified simulation — Operation Crimson Tide, a tabletop exercise run at the Naval War College six weeks ago. The simulation modeled a full-scale confrontation with Iran and the results were sobering: heavy casualties, two ships damaged, one sunk, and a conflict that lasted fourteen months. The public is alarmed. Gold prices spike. Your own staff is reading the article on their phones with expressions that range from anger to fear. The Pentagon's spokesperson is demanding guidance. The leaker is still unknown.
consequences: You downplay it and investigate. 'Wargames model worst cases — that's their purpose,' your spokesperson says. The story dominates the news cycle for three days while the leak investigation produces nothing. The public's memory is short, but the seed of doubt has been planted. People are now imagining body bags.

You use the leak as a weapon. In a press conference, you hold up the simulation report. 'This is exactly why we need de-escalation,' you say. 'This is what war looks like. We must find another way.' Public opinion shifts measurably toward diplomacy. The hawks are outflanked by their own wargame.

You double down on readiness. 'We train for worst cases so they never happen,' you say, your jaw set. 'Our military is prepared for any contingency.' Hawks applaud. Military families feel reassured. Iran's intelligence analysts read between the lines and conclude you are preparing for exactly the scenario the wargame modeled.

stena_imperative.scene: The tactical display lights up with six hostile contacts converging on a single green icon. The Stena Imperative, a Swedish-flagged tanker, is under siege. IRGC gunboats — Heydar-class, the fastest combat boats on earth — are closing from three directions at fifty knots. The tanker's captain is on the distress frequency, his voice cracking with fear: 'Any warship, any warship, this is Stena Imperative, we are being approached by Iranian military vessels, request immediate assistance.' USS McFaul, DDG-74, is twelve minutes away at flank speed. An F-35 from the Eisenhower is eight minutes out. The clock is running, and the IRGC boats are not slowing down.
stena_imperative.urgency: The IRGC boats are four minutes from boarding range. McFaul is still eight minutes out. There is no time for a committee.
consequences: You give the order: full speed intercept, weapons hot. The McFaul's turbines scream as she comes to flank speed, her bow cutting a white scar across the dark water. Overhead, the F-35 drops through the clouds and immediately engages an Iranian Shahed drone on a collision course with the tanker. The explosion is visible for miles. IRGC boats scatter as the destroyer's fire-control radar paints them. The Stena Imperative escapes. The tanker captain weeps with relief on the radio.

You order McFaul to intercept but keep weapons tight. The destroyer positions herself between the tanker and the gunboats, her hull a steel wall. For forty minutes, the standoff holds — American sailors on deck, fingers near triggers, watching Iranian boats circle at two hundred meters. Finally, one by one, the IRGC boats peel away. The Stena Imperative sails free. No shots fired. No one sleeps well tonight.

You order monitoring only. The tactical feed shows what happens next in high-definition: IRGC commandos board the Stena Imperative, replacing the Swedish flag with Iranian colors. The crew is ordered below. The video — captured by surveillance drones you ordered not to act — goes viral within the hour. 'Where was the Navy?' trends globally. The answer sits heavy in the room.

insurance_crisis.scene: The call from Lloyd's of London comes to your Treasury Secretary at 6 AM: all war risk coverage in the Persian Gulf has been cancelled, effective immediately. The ripple effect is instant and devastating. Without insurance, no tanker can legally transit. Without tankers, no oil moves. Without oil, the global economy begins to seize. Within hours, the market impact is visible: futures spike, shipping companies announce 'indefinite suspension' of Gulf operations, and your inbox fills with urgent messages from every energy-importing nation on earth. The maritime insurance market — a system older than America itself — has just declared the Strait of Hormuz uninsurable.
consequences: You authorize a twenty-billion-dollar government-backed insurance program. The announcement is made jointly with the Treasury Secretary and the Secretary of Transportation. Chubb signs as lead underwriter within the day, backstopped by the full faith and credit of the United States. Tankers begin scheduling transit within forty-eight hours. The money is staggering, but the alternative — a closed strait — is worse.

You pressure the insurers to resume coverage. Phone calls from the Treasury Secretary, meetings with Lloyd's leadership, implied threats about regulatory scrutiny. The arm-twisting produces limited results: coverage resumes at three hundred percent normal premiums. Some tankers resume transit. Others decide the math does not work. The strait is half-open, which may be worse than fully closed.

You let the market sort itself out. No intervention, no backstop, no pressure. Transit drops seventy percent within a week. Over 150 ships sit at anchor outside the strait, their crews idle, their cargo undelivered. Gas hits six dollars a gallon in the American heartland. The free market has spoken, and its verdict is that the Strait of Hormuz is not worth the risk.

nuclear_breakout.scene: The CIA Director's face is gray as old concrete when she walks into the Situation Room. She lays a single satellite photograph on the table — Fordow enrichment facility, buried deep in a mountain near Qom. The centrifuge cascades are spinning at ninety percent enrichment. 'They're weeks from a weapon, possibly days,' she says. The room falls silent. Every option on the table leads somewhere terrible.
consequences: You give the order. The B-2 Spirit lifts off from Whiteman Air Force Base carrying thirty-thousand-pound bunker busters. Twelve hours later, the mountain above Fordow collapses inward. The world watches in stunned silence. You have just committed an act of war against a sovereign nation's nuclear program. The consequences will unfold for decades.

You pick up the phone and call the IAEA Director General. Within hours, a team of inspectors is en route to Tehran, armed with your intelligence package and a Security Council mandate. It is a gamble — trusting verification over force. The inspectors will either prove the threat or become hostages to it.

You propose a deal. Direct talks, sanctions relief in exchange for dismantlement, a path that your own party will call surrender. The diplomatic cable goes out through back channels. You are betting that Iran wants survival more than it wants the bomb. History will judge whether you were right.

abqaiq_redux.scene: The attack comes at dawn, a swarm of cruise missiles and drones converging on Saudi Arabia's Abqaiq processing facility from multiple vectors. The facility handles five percent of global oil supply. Satellite imagery shows plumes of black smoke rising ten thousand feet into the Arabian sky. Saudi air defenses engaged some of the inbound weapons, but enough got through. Oil futures are already spiking.
consequences: You authorize retaliatory strikes against the launch sites. Tomahawks arc across the Persian Gulf, hitting IRGC positions along the Iranian coast. The strikes are precise, devastating, and immediately escalatory. The price of oil jumps another twenty dollars a barrel before markets even open.

You work the phones, calling Riyadh, London, and the UN Secretary General in rapid succession. A coalition response, measured and unified, begins to take shape. The Saudis want blood. You offer them something more useful — a plan.
You hold your fire. The intelligence is murky, the attribution uncertain enough to give you cover. Markets panic, pundits howl, but you refuse to be stampeded into a wider war by a single attack.

houthi_restart.scene: CENTCOM's morning brief hits your desk with a red border. Houthi forces in Yemen have resumed attacks on commercial shipping in the Red Sea, launching anti-ship ballistic missiles at two tankers transiting the Bab el-Mandeb strait. One missile was intercepted by a Navy destroyer. The other struck a Panamanian-flagged chemical tanker, which is now listing and leaking cargo into the sea. The Houthis are Iran's most unpredictable proxy, and they smell opportunity in the Hormuz crisis.
consequences: You order strikes on Houthi launch sites and weapons depots in Yemen. The campaign begins within hours — cruise missiles slamming into mountain hideouts and coastal positions. The attacks degrade Houthi capability but scatter their fighters into the civilian population.

You coordinate with regional allies to establish a multinational naval escort through the Red Sea. Additional destroyers are repositioned, and intelligence sharing with Saudi and Emirati forces increases. It costs money and stretches your fleet thinner, but shipping lanes reopen.

You issue a diplomatic warning through Omani intermediaries, threatening consequences if attacks continue. The Houthis respond with a propaganda video showing their missile launchers. The attacks do not stop.

mine_laying_detected.scene: A P-8 Poseidon maritime patrol aircraft spots them first — small boats operating under cover of darkness in the narrowest section of the Strait. Infrared cameras capture the unmistakable silhouette of naval mines being rolled off the sterns. By morning, your minesweeper crews confirm it: Iran is mining the Strait of Hormuz. Every tanker captain in the Gulf is now recalculating risk. Insurance rates triple before lunch.
