consequences: You authorize an immediate mine-clearing operation backed by naval air support. Helicopters sweep the shipping lanes while minesweepers inch forward through contested waters. It is dangerous, painstaking work, and every hour the lanes remain closed costs the global economy millions.

You assemble an international minesweeping coalition, calling in British, French, and Japanese assets to share the burden. The multinational approach is slower but spreads the risk and the political exposure across multiple governments.

You declare a maritime exclusion zone and announce that any vessel detected laying mines will be destroyed on sight. The declaration is a line drawn in salt water. Whether Iran crosses it will define the next phase of this crisis.

drone_carrier.scene: Naval intelligence has located it — a converted cargo vessel operating in international waters south of Bandar Abbas, its hold packed with dozens of armed drones. Your analysts call it a 'drone carrier,' a floating launch platform capable of saturating your fleet's air defenses. The vessel is flying no flag, its transponder dark. Satellite imagery shows deck hatches being opened in preparation for a launch sequence.
consequences: You order a preemptive strike. A pair of F/A-18s descend on the vessel and put two precision-guided munitions through its hull. The ship breaks apart and sinks in fourteen minutes. Iran denies any connection. The drone swarm never launches.

You shadow the vessel with a submarine and deploy electronic warfare assets to jam its communications. Your intelligence teams monitor every transmission, building a comprehensive picture of Iran's drone warfare doctrine without firing a shot.

You share the intelligence with allies and bring the evidence to the UN Security Council, framing Iran's drone carrier as a threat to international shipping. The diplomatic approach takes time, but it builds the coalition you need for whatever comes next.

china_oil_deal.scene: Your trade representative bursts into the morning briefing with intercepts showing that China and Iran have signed a secret oil-for-infrastructure agreement. Beijing will purchase Iranian crude at a steep discount, paid in yuan, effectively creating a sanctions-proof lifeline for Tehran's economy. The deal undermines your entire economic pressure strategy. Beijing's ambassador requests an urgent meeting.
consequences: You impose secondary sanctions on Chinese entities involved in the deal, threatening to cut them off from the American financial system. Beijing retaliates with tariffs. The economic escalation spirals, but Iran's oil revenue takes a hit.

You quietly negotiate with Beijing, offering trade concessions in exchange for reduced Iranian oil purchases. The haggling is ugly and transactional, but you chip away at Iran's economic lifeline without a public confrontation.

You let the deal stand, calculating that antagonizing China while managing Iran would stretch your leverage too thin. Tehran gets its money. Beijing gets its oil. Your sanctions regime develops a leak that will only grow.

oman_talks.scene: The Omani foreign minister arrives at your hotel suite in Muscat with a handwritten letter from Iran's foreign minister. The calligraphy is elegant, the language formal, but the message is unmistakable: Tehran is willing to negotiate. You are sitting in the same city where the back-channel talks that led to the original Iran nuclear deal began years ago. The ghosts of past diplomacy hover in the warm night air.
consequences: You agree to direct talks. Your envoy meets the Iranian delegation in a villa overlooking the Gulf of Oman. The conversations are tense, halting, full of distrust, but they are conversations. For the first time in weeks, someone on the other side is listening.

You send a counter-proposal through the Omanis, adding conditions that you know Iran will resist. The move buys time and tests Tehran's seriousness without committing you to anything. The diplomatic dance continues at arm's length.

You reject the overture and leak it to the press. The headline — 'Iran Seeks Talks as Crisis Deepens' — plays well domestically but slams the door on diplomacy. The Omani foreign minister returns to Muscat empty-handed and disappointed.

asian_energy_crisis.scene: The cascading effects of the Strait closure have reached Asia. Japan, South Korea, and India — nations that depend on Gulf oil for the majority of their energy imports — are facing rolling blackouts and industrial shutdowns. Tokyo's ambassador delivers a blunt message: 'Resolve this, or we will find our own solution.' The global economy is buckling, and the pressure to act is coming from every direction.
consequences: You authorize emergency oil shipments from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve and coordinate releases with allied nations. Tankers laden with American crude set course for Asian ports. It is expensive and depletes your reserves, but it buys goodwill and time.

You tell the Asian allies to increase pressure on Iran directly, leveraging their own economic relationships with Tehran. The approach spreads the burden but risks fracturing the coalition if each nation cuts its own deal.

You propose an emergency energy summit, bringing affected nations together to coordinate a unified response. The summit builds solidarity and produces a joint statement, though critics note that statements do not power factories.

gas_price_crisis.scene: The number on the gas station signs back home has become a political weapon. Six dollars, seven dollars, climbing toward eight. Congressional switchboards are melting. Truckers are blockading refineries. Your domestic policy advisor lays the polling data on your desk like a coroner presenting an autopsy. Your approval rating is in freefall, tied directly to the price at the pump.
consequences: You release petroleum reserves and suspend the federal gas tax, providing immediate relief at the pump. The fiscal cost is staggering, but the political bleeding stops. For now.

You go on national television and level with the American people, explaining the strategic stakes and asking for patience. You frame the sacrifice as patriotic duty. Some rally to the flag. Others change the channel.
You pressure oil companies to reduce their margins and accelerate domestic drilling permits. The industry pushes back, but the optics of 'doing something' blunt the worst of the political damage.

war_powers_vote.scene: The Speaker of the House has scheduled a War Powers Resolution vote for tomorrow. The debate on the floor is already bitter — hawks accusing doves of weakness, doves accusing hawks of warmongering. Your legislative team counts votes through the night. The outcome will either authorize expanded military operations or force a drawdown that could embolden Iran. Either way, the decision is no longer yours alone.
consequences: You lobby hard against the resolution, spending political capital to maintain executive authority. Phone calls, favors, threats — the machinery of presidential persuasion runs at full power. You win the vote, but the margin is razor-thin and the resentment lingers.

You work with congressional leadership to craft a compromise resolution that preserves your flexibility while giving Congress a voice. The bipartisan approach costs you some freedom of action but strengthens your domestic mandate.

You publicly welcome the congressional debate, framing it as democracy in action during a time of crisis. The gesture earns praise from the press and builds a broader coalition of support, even if the final resolution constrains your options.

tanker_war_echoes.scene: The parallels are impossible to ignore. Your military historians have pulled the files from the 1987-88 Tanker War, and the similarities are haunting — Iranian attacks on commercial shipping, American convoy escorts, the constant threat of escalation. Admiral Hayes presents Operation Earnest Will as a template. 'It worked then,' he says. 'It can work now.' But you know how that story ended — with the USS Vincennes shooting down an Iranian airliner.
consequences: You authorize a full convoy escort system, assigning warships to shepherd tankers through the Strait. The operation is massive — every available destroyer and frigate reassigned to escort duty. Oil begins to flow again, but your fleet is stretched to breaking and every transit is a potential flashpoint.

You implement a partial escort program, prioritizing the most critical tanker routes while leaving secondary traffic to navigate independently. The half-measure satisfies no one completely but keeps your forces flexible.

You reject the convoy model and focus on deterrence instead, positioning overwhelming naval force at the chokepoints without direct escort duties. The approach is less protective but avoids tying your ships to slow-moving tankers.

cyber_port_attack.scene: Bandar Abbas port's computer systems go dark at 0200 local time. Your cyber command reports a sophisticated attack — Iranian origin, targeting the port's automated cargo handling and navigation systems. Ships in the harbor cannot dock or depart. The attack is a signal: Iran can disrupt the Strait without firing a single shot. Your own cyber teams are already mapping potential counterstrike targets.
consequences: You authorize a retaliatory cyber operation against Iran's military command and control networks. Your hackers penetrate deep, disabling radar systems and communications along the Iranian coast. It is invisible warfare, deniable and devastating.

You dispatch a cyber recovery team to help restore the port's systems while hardening your own naval communications against similar attacks. The defensive approach is prudent but does nothing to deter the next strike.

You bring the evidence of Iran's cyber attack to the international community, building a case for coordinated sanctions against Iran's cyber warfare units. The attribution is solid, and the diplomatic damage to Tehran is real.

second_carrier.scene: The Pentagon proposes deploying a second carrier strike group to the Persian Gulf. The logistics are staggering — repositioning the USS Gerald Ford from the Mediterranean, accelerating maintenance on escorts, rerouting supply chains. Your defense secretary argues it will send an unmistakable signal of American resolve. Your budget director argues it will send an unmistakable signal to your creditors.
consequences: You approve the deployment. Within days, two American carrier strike groups are operating in the Gulf simultaneously — a concentration of naval power not seen since the Iraq War. Iran's admirals quietly begin repositioning their forces. The deterrent effect is real, but so is the cost.

You approve a partial reinforcement, sending additional destroyers and submarines without the second carrier. The augmented force provides meaningful capability without the political and financial weight of a dual-carrier deployment.

You decline the deployment, judging that the current naval presence is sufficient and that further escalation could trigger the very conflict you are trying to prevent. The Pentagon accepts the decision. Grudgingly.

truth_social_armada.scene: You wake to find that a senior official has posted on social media about sending 'the greatest armada the world has ever seen' to the Persian Gulf. The post has gone viral. Allies are confused, adversaries are calculating, and your communications team is in damage-control mode. The markets have already reacted — defense stocks are surging, oil futures climbing. Your national security advisor is standing in your doorway, looking like someone who has swallowed a grenade.
consequences: You double down, turning the rhetoric into reality by announcing a major naval buildup. If the world thinks you are sending an armada, then by God you will send one. The bluster becomes policy, and Iran watches a stream of warships enter the Gulf.

You walk it back, issuing a carefully worded statement that reframes the post as 'aspirational language about American strength.' The diplomatic community exhales. Your credibility takes a dent, but the immediate crisis passes.
You channel the attention into a diplomatic initiative, using the world's eyes as leverage to call for multilateral negotiations. The chaos becomes an opportunity, if you can steer it.

iran_internal_struggle.scene: Your intelligence analysts have identified a fracture within the Iranian regime. The IRGC hardliners under General Tangsiri are pushing for maximum confrontation, while the foreign ministry faction led by Araghchi advocates negotiation. Intercepted communications suggest the Supreme Leader has not yet chosen a side. This internal struggle is your opening — or your trap.
consequences: You reach out through back channels to the moderate faction, offering quiet assurances that de-escalation will be met with sanctions relief. The overture is risky — if the hardliners discover it, the moderates are finished. But if it works, you may end this without a shot.

You tighten sanctions further, targeting the IRGC's business empire specifically, hoping to empower the moderates by weakening their rivals. The economic pressure is a scalpel, aimed at the regime's internal power dynamics.
You maintain your current posture, unwilling to tip your hand while the internal struggle plays out. Sometimes the best move is to do nothing and let your adversary's contradictions do the work.

fast_boat_swarm.scene: CENTCOM reports a mass sortie of IRGC fast boats from multiple Iranian ports simultaneously. Dozens of armed speedboats are converging on the shipping lanes in a coordinated swarm pattern. Your fleet commanders are requesting weapons-free authorization. The radar screens look like a kicked anthill. This is the scenario your war planners have war-gamed a hundred times, and it is happening now.
consequences: You authorize weapons-free. The engagement is short and brutal — American firepower against Iranian numbers. Fast boats explode and capsize across miles of ocean. The swarm is broken, but the body count is staggering, and the footage will be on Al Jazeera within the hour.

You authorize warning shots and aggressive maneuvering, hoping to disperse the swarm without a full engagement. Your destroyer captains play a deadly game of chicken at forty knots, their wakes cutting through the formation.

You order your fleet to fall back and form a defensive perimeter around the tanker convoy, refusing to be drawn into a melee that could scatter your forces. The swarm probes your defenses, testing for weakness.

hezbollah_front.scene: Hezbollah has opened a second front. Rockets are falling on the US Embassy compound in Baghdad, and your forces at Al-Tanf garrison in Syria are under sustained mortar fire. The proxy network is activating across the region, stretching your attention and your resources. The Iranian strategy is becoming clear — they will fight you everywhere except Iran.
consequences: You strike Hezbollah positions in Lebanon and Syria simultaneously, sending a message that proxy warfare has a price. The strikes are precise but the political fallout in Beirut is severe. The Lebanese government condemns the attacks.

You work through diplomatic channels, pressuring Lebanon's government and engaging with European allies to isolate Hezbollah politically. The approach is slower but avoids widening the war to a third country.
You absorb the attacks, reinforcing your positions but refusing to be drawn into a multi-front conflict. The proxy strikes continue, but your strategic focus remains on the Strait.

cape_route_crisis.scene: Shipping companies are rerouting around the Cape of Good Hope to avoid the Strait entirely. The alternative route adds two weeks and massive fuel costs to every tanker voyage. The global supply chain is warping under the pressure — delivery times extending, costs skyrocketing, industries that depend on just-in-time logistics grinding to a halt. Your economic advisors present models showing a global recession within sixty days if the Strait remains contested.
consequences: You subsidize the Cape route transits, offering insurance guarantees and fuel cost offsets to keep goods flowing. The price tag is enormous, but it keeps the global economy on life support while you work the strategic problem.

You escalate naval operations in the Strait, arguing that the only solution to the shipping crisis is to reopen the direct route by force if necessary. The military option carries risks, but the economic alternative is already a disaster.

You convene an emergency G7 summit to coordinate the economic response, pushing for burden-sharing and joint action on strategic petroleum reserves. The summit produces commitments, though implementation will lag behind promises.

minab_school_strike.scene: The images arrive from Iranian state television and spread across social media within minutes. A school in the coastal city of Minab, reduced to rubble. Children's backpacks visible in the debris. Iran claims it was an American airstrike. Your targeting analysts insist the coordinates were a military communications relay. The truth hardly matters now — the world is watching, and the footage is devastating.
consequences: You immediately acknowledge the strike and announce a full investigation, suspending air operations in the area pending review. The admission costs you politically at home, but the international community responds to the transparency.

You deny responsibility, pointing to Iranian air defense debris and the regime's history of staging propaganda. Your intelligence supports the denial, but the images are more powerful than any briefing paper.

You halt all offensive operations and call for an immediate ceasefire, using the tragedy as a pivot point. The gesture is dramatic and risky — your military commanders warn that pausing operations concedes initiative to Iran.

mojtaba_succession.scene: Your intelligence briefer delivers the morning's bombshell quietly, almost casually. The Supreme Leader's health is failing, and his son Mojtaba Khamenei is positioning himself to inherit power. The IRGC generals are divided — some support Mojtaba, others see him as a weak pretender. Iran's internal politics are about to become the most dangerous variable in your crisis equation.
consequences: You instruct your intelligence services to monitor the succession closely, redirecting satellite coverage and SIGINT assets toward Tehran's power centers. Understanding who controls Iran tomorrow is worth more than any missile strike today.

You publicly comment on the leadership transition, framing it as an opportunity for a 'new chapter' in relations. The statement is calculated to unsettle the hardliners and encourage the moderates. Iran's state media erupts in fury.

You accelerate military pressure, calculating that a regime in transition is a regime vulnerable to coercion. The strikes intensify as Tehran's power brokers look inward, distracted by succession politics.

joe_kent_resignation.scene: The resignation letter lands on your desk at 0600, leaked to the press by 0615. A senior national security official — a decorated combat veteran with deep credibility in the defense community — has quit, publicly condemning your handling of the Iran crisis. The letter is devastating, detailed, and already trending. Cable news has him booked for every primetime show tonight.
consequences: You let the resignation stand and refuse to engage publicly. Your press secretary issues a terse statement thanking the official for their service. The news cycle churns, but you give it nothing to feed on. The story fades in days, though the damage to morale lingers.
You respond with a measured statement acknowledging disagreements while emphasizing the complexity of the crisis. You promote a respected deputy to fill the role, signaling stability and continuity.

You address the nation directly, using the resignation as a catalyst for a broader strategic recalibration. You announce a policy review and invite congressional consultation, turning a crisis of confidence into a display of leadership.

gcc_attacks.scene: The attacks are simultaneous and coordinated. Ballistic missiles strike a Saudi military base, a drone swarm targets a UAE oil terminal, and rockets hit a Bahraini naval facility. The Gulf Cooperation Council states are screaming for an American response. Your allies' capitals are burning, and the pressure to act is unlike anything you have felt. The Gulf monarchs are on the phone, one after another, each more furious than the last.
consequences: You launch a massive retaliatory strike package against Iranian military targets — airbases, missile batteries, naval installations. The operation is the largest American military action since the Iraq War. The Gulf allies cheer. The rest of the world holds its breath.

You coordinate a joint GCC-American response, sharing targeting data and command structures with allied forces. The coalition strike is smaller but politically powerful, demonstrating unified resolve against Iranian aggression.

You urge restraint, arguing that a measured response will prevent further escalation. The Gulf monarchs are incredulous. Your phone calls become shorter, the tones colder. The alliance strains under the weight of unmet expectations.

iris_dena_aftermath.scene: The IRIS Dena, an Iranian frigate, lies crippled in the Gulf of Oman after an engagement with coalition naval forces. Rescue operations are underway — Iranian sailors in the water, some wounded, all of them pawns in a game played at much higher altitudes. The footage of American sailors pulling Iranian sailors from the oil-slicked water is simultaneously a humanitarian triumph and a propaganda minefield.
consequences: You order the rescued sailors returned immediately through Red Cross channels, making the humanitarian gesture as public and clean as possible. The world sees American hands pulling Iranian sailors from the sea. Tehran's propaganda machine stutters.

You hold the rescued sailors as bargaining chips, leveraging their return in exchange for concessions on detained tanker crews. The calculation is cold but pragmatic. International law experts on your staff exchange nervous glances.

You stage a media event around the rescue, inviting embedded journalists to document American sailors providing medical care to their Iranian counterparts. The images cut through the fog of war with unexpected clarity.

three_carriers.scene: The Joint Chiefs present a plan to surge a third carrier strike group into the theater. Three carriers, their escorts, their air wings — a floating fortress capable of projecting more firepower than most nations possess. The deployment would be the largest concentration of American naval power in a generation. Your Secretary of Defense calls it 'overwhelming deterrence.' Your Treasury Secretary calls it 'overwhelming expenditure.'
consequences: You approve the triple-carrier deployment. The sight of three American flight decks operating in concert sends a signal that reverberates from Tehran to Beijing. Iran's navy retreats to port. The deterrent works, but at a cost that will echo through next year's defense budget.
You approve two carriers and supplement with submarine and bomber assets, achieving comparable striking power with a smaller surface footprint. The approach is less visible but arguably more lethal.

You decline the surge, judging the current force sufficient and the fiscal exposure unacceptable. You redirect the funds toward diplomatic initiatives and economic tools. The Pentagon drafts a strongly worded memo of dissent.

china_mediation_real.scene: Beijing has made its move. China's foreign minister arrives in Tehran with a comprehensive peace proposal, brokered in quiet consultations with both sides. The plan calls for mutual de-escalation, sanctions relief tied to verifiable milestones, and Chinese peacekeepers in the Strait. It is elegant, ambitious, and designed to position Beijing as the indispensable power broker of the twenty-first century. Your allies are intrigued. Your hawks are appalled.
consequences: You engage with the Chinese proposal in good faith, sending your own envoy to Beijing for trilateral discussions. The talks are grueling — three languages, three sets of interests, three definitions of victory. But for the first time, there is a framework.
You reject the Chinese framework but counter with your own proposal, maintaining American primacy in Gulf security while addressing some of Beijing's concerns. The diplomatic chess match intensifies.

You publicly dismiss Chinese mediation as a power grab, rallying allies around an American-led solution. Beijing's face is bruised, and the channel closes. Your domestic audience applauds, but a potential off-ramp has vanished.

russia_intel_leak.scene: A Russian intelligence operative has defected to your embassy in Tbilisi, carrying a trove of classified documents detailing Moscow's military support to Iran. The files reveal weapons shipments, technical advisors, satellite intelligence sharing — a shadow war conducted through Iranian proxies. The defector is credible, the documents verifiable. You have in your hands the evidence to reshape the entire geopolitical narrative.
consequences: You declassify the Russian intelligence and present it to the world. The revelation of Moscow's shadow support for Iran reshapes the diplomatic landscape. Russia scrambles to deny, but the documents speak for themselves.

You keep the intelligence classified, using it to quietly pressure Moscow into reducing its support for Iran. The back-channel diplomacy is delicate — threatening without provoking, leveraging without exposing your source.

You share the intelligence selectively with key allies, building a quiet coalition of informed nations without the public spectacle of a confrontation with Russia. The approach is measured, the effect cumulative.

nowruz_ceasefire.scene: The Omani intermediary arrives with an unexpected proposal: Iran is offering a ceasefire timed to Nowruz, the Persian New Year. The gesture carries deep cultural significance — a fresh start, a new beginning. Your Iran experts see it as a genuine olive branch from the moderate faction. Your military advisors see it as a ploy to reposition forces. Both could be right.
consequences: You accept the Nowruz ceasefire and order a full stand-down of offensive operations. The silence that falls over the Gulf is profound and fragile. Every hour that passes without violence becomes an argument for peace. You hold your breath and wait.

You accept a limited ceasefire covering the Strait itself while maintaining defensive operations elsewhere. The half-measure satisfies neither doves nor hawks, but it reduces the immediate risk of catastrophe while preserving your options.
You reject the ceasefire, arguing that Iran is using the holiday as cover for military repositioning. The strikes continue through Nowruz. Iran's moderates lose their last argument for diplomacy.

hezbollah_ceasefire_break.scene: The ceasefire is barely hours old when Hezbollah launches a barrage of rockets from southern Lebanon toward a US logistics base in northern Iraq. The attack kills no one — the base was evacuated as a precaution — but the violation is brazen. Tehran claims Hezbollah acts independently. Nobody in your Situation Room believes that for a second.
consequences: You strike back hard, targeting Hezbollah weapons depots and command nodes across Lebanon and Syria. The response is disproportionate by design — a message that ceasefire violations carry an exponential cost.

You respond through diplomatic channels, giving Iran forty-eight hours to rein in its proxy or face consequences. The ultimatum is public and binding. Tehran's response will reveal whether the moderate faction has any real control.

You absorb the attack and maintain the broader ceasefire framework, treating Hezbollah's violation as a spoiler action by hardliners trying to torpedo negotiations. The restraint is agonizing but calculated.

houthi_escalation.scene: Houthi forces in Yemen have escalated dramatically, launching their most sophisticated attack yet — a coordinated barrage of anti-ship ballistic missiles and explosive-laden drone boats targeting a convoy of tankers in the Red Sea. Your destroyers intercept most of the incoming, but one tanker takes a hit and is ablaze. The Bab el-Mandeb strait, the world's second-most critical oil chokepoint, is now as dangerous as Hormuz.
consequences: You launch a sustained air campaign against Houthi military infrastructure in Yemen, deploying B-1 bombers alongside carrier-based aircraft. The strikes are devastating but the humanitarian implications in war-torn Yemen are severe.

You expand the naval escort program to cover both the Strait of Hormuz and the Red Sea approaches, stretching your fleet across two theaters. The logistical challenge is immense but the shipping lanes must stay open.

You work through Saudi and Emirati intermediaries to negotiate a separate Houthi stand-down, offering indirect concessions on the Yemen conflict in exchange for a cessation of maritime attacks. The diplomacy is messy and morally ambiguous.

kc135_crash.scene: A KC-135 aerial refueling tanker has gone down in the Arabian Sea during a routine mission. Four crew members are missing. Search and rescue operations are underway, but the crash site is in contested waters. Iran's navy is in the area. The cause is unknown — mechanical failure, hostile action, or something in between. The families are being notified. The press is asking questions you cannot yet answer.
consequences: You go public immediately with the facts as you know them, honoring the crew and committing to a full investigation. The transparency earns trust even as it exposes uncertainty. The families deserve the truth, even an incomplete one.

You order a pause in air operations pending investigation, pulling tanker aircraft from their refueling orbits until the cause is determined. The operational impact is significant — strike range shrinks, sortie rates drop — but you will not lose more crews to a preventable cause.

You classify the incident and accelerate operations, refusing to let a single loss dictate the tempo of your campaign. The crews understand risk. The mission continues. Your critics will call it callous. Your commanders call it necessary.

war_crimes_tribunal.scene: The International Criminal Court has announced a preliminary investigation into potential war crimes committed during the Strait crisis. The investigation targets both sides, but American actions — the airstrikes, the blockade enforcement, the naval engagements — are under particular scrutiny. Your legal team is divided on the exposure. The political implications are enormous.
consequences: You reject the ICC's jurisdiction outright, citing longstanding American policy and threatening sanctions against the court. The defiance plays well domestically but isolates you from European allies who support the tribunal.

You cooperate with the investigation, providing evidence that you believe will exonerate American forces while exposing Iranian violations. The approach is risky — you are submitting to a process you do not control — but it demonstrates commitment to the rule of law.

You engage with the ICC selectively, cooperating on specific inquiries while challenging the court's broader mandate. The middle path satisfies no one perfectly but avoids the extremes of defiance and submission.

mojtaba_consolidation.scene: Mojtaba Khamenei has consolidated power faster than anyone predicted. The IRGC generals who opposed him have been quietly retired or reassigned. Tehran's new power structure is taking shape, and it is unclear whether Mojtaba will prove more moderate or more extreme than his father. Your intelligence is contradictory — some sources say he wants a deal, others say he is preparing for war. The uncertainty is paralyzing.
consequences: You extend a diplomatic overture to the new regime, proposing direct talks without preconditions. The offer is a test of Mojtaba's intentions and a signal that America is willing to deal with whoever holds power in Tehran.

You increase pressure immediately, calculating that a new leader needs a demonstration of strength to consolidate internally and that showing weakness now will be exploited. Sanctions tighten, naval patrols intensify.

You wait and watch, committing additional intelligence resources to understanding Mojtaba's inner circle while maintaining your current posture. The patience is deliberate — you will not make the first move until you understand the board.

al_udeid_attack.scene: Ballistic missiles are inbound toward Al Udeid Air Base in Qatar, the nerve center of US air operations in the Gulf. Patriot batteries fire in rapid sequence, streaks of light arcing into the night sky. Most of the missiles are intercepted, but fragments rain down on the flight line. One hangar is burning. The base commander reports casualties. This is the first direct Iranian attack on an American military installation.
consequences: You authorize an immediate retaliatory strike against the missile launch sites and the IRGC command center that ordered the attack. The response is swift, precise, and designed to demonstrate that attacking American soil — even rented soil — carries an unacceptable price.

You reinforce base defenses and relocate critical assets to dispersed positions, absorbing the attack while hardening your posture. The defensive response is paired with a diplomatic ultimatum delivered through Swiss intermediaries.

You disperse air operations across multiple bases in the region, reducing the vulnerability of any single installation. The logistical complexity increases dramatically, but the strategic resilience improves.

indian_energy_crisis.scene: India's prime minister calls you directly, bypassing the usual diplomatic channels. The subcontinent is running out of oil. Power cuts are spreading, factories are shutting down, and street protests are erupting in Mumbai and Delhi. India imports eighty percent of its oil through the Strait of Hormuz. 'My country is dying slowly,' the prime minister says. 'I need you to fix this.'
consequences: You authorize emergency oil shipments to India, diverting tankers from other routes and tapping strategic reserves. The gesture cements an alliance and eases the humanitarian crisis, though your own reserves dwindle further.

You propose a joint Indian-American diplomatic initiative to reopen the Strait, leveraging India's unique position as a nation with ties to both Iran and the West. The partnership is novel and promising.

You tell India that the crisis will end when Iran stands down, and that international pressure on Tehran is the fastest path to relief. The message is honest but cold. India begins exploring alternative energy partnerships with Russia.

carrier_near_miss.scene: The USS Eisenhower's combat information center erupts in alarm. An Iranian anti-ship ballistic missile has splashed into the water less than five hundred meters from the carrier's hull. Close enough to feel the shockwave on the flight deck. Close enough that if the guidance had been fractionally better, you would be dealing with the worst naval disaster since World War Two. Your admiral is requesting permission to destroy every missile battery on the Iranian coast.
consequences: You authorize a devastating strike package against Iran's coastal anti-ship missile infrastructure. Tomahawks and precision-guided munitions rain down on batteries, radars, and command bunkers along the Iranian littoral. The coast goes quiet. The escalation does not.

You pull the carrier group back to a safer distance, conceding sea space in exchange for survivability. The tactical retreat is prudent but the optics are painful — an American carrier retreating from Iranian missiles makes headlines around the world.

You reinforce the carrier's defensive screen with additional Aegis destroyers and deploy electronic warfare aircraft to jam Iranian targeting radars. The defensive investment is expensive but keeps your carrier in the fight.

iran_moderate_coup.scene: The intelligence arrives in fragments, pieced together from SIGINT intercepts and human sources in Tehran. A faction of Iranian moderates — military officers, business leaders, reformist politicians — is planning to move against the hardliners. They want to know if America will support a political transition. The request comes with a warning: if this fails, the hardliners will be more dangerous than ever.
consequences: You authorize covert support for the moderate faction, providing intelligence, communications equipment, and quiet assurances through intermediaries. The operation is the highest-stakes gamble of your career. If it succeeds, the crisis ends. If it fails, it deepens catastrophically.

You provide limited intelligence support while maintaining plausible deniability, giving the moderates enough help to improve their odds without committing America to the outcome. The calibrated approach limits your exposure but also limits your influence.

You refuse to get involved, judging that American interference in Iranian domestic politics will only discredit the moderates and unite the country against foreign meddling. The moderates are on their own.

kc135_crew_rescue.scene: Your downed KC-135 crew has been located — alive, floating in life rafts in the Arabian Sea, sixty nautical miles from the Iranian coast. IRGC fast boats have been spotted heading toward the same coordinates. A Navy helicopter rescue team is thirty minutes out. The race is on, and the lives of four American servicemembers hang in the balance.
consequences: You authorize the rescue mission with full air cover, scrambling fighters to establish a protective perimeter around the crash site. The helicopters go in fast and low. Iranian fast boats turn back when they see the air cover. Your crew comes home.

You attempt to coordinate with Iran through the maritime deconfliction channel, requesting safe passage for the rescue. The channel crackles with static and delay. Every minute of negotiation is a minute your crew spends in the water.

You leak the crew's location and the IRGC's approach to international media, creating a global audience for whatever happens next. The spotlight forces Iran's hand — taking prisoners on live television is a different calculus than doing it in the dark.

european_split.scene: The European Union is fracturing over the Iran crisis. France and Germany are calling for immediate negotiations and a suspension of military operations. Britain stands with you, but the Eastern European nations are distracted by their own security concerns. The transatlantic alliance, the bedrock of American foreign policy for eighty years, is cracking along the fault lines of the Strait of Hormuz.
consequences: You dismiss European criticism and press forward unilaterally, calculating that American military power does not require European permission. The alliance frays further, but your operational freedom is preserved.

You fly to Brussels for an emergency NATO summit, spending two days in bruising negotiations to rebuild consensus. The communique that emerges is weaker than you wanted but stronger than your critics expected.
You exploit the split, cutting bilateral deals with supportive nations while publicly shaming the dissenters. The approach is divisive by design, rewarding loyalty and punishing defection.

backchannel_progress.scene: The Omani intermediary returns with news from Tehran. The back-channel talks have produced a framework — tentative, fragile, full of ambiguities that both sides are pretending not to notice. The moderates in Tehran are offering a phased de-escalation: reduced IRGC naval activity in exchange for partial sanctions relief. Your hawks call it a trap. Your diplomats call it a breakthrough. The truth is probably somewhere in between.
consequences: You accept the framework and begin implementation immediately, ordering a partial naval drawdown as a show of good faith. The Strait quiets. Oil begins to flow. The ceasefire holds with the delicacy of wet paper, but it holds.

You counter-propose, pushing for broader terms that include proxy forces and the nuclear program. The Iranians balk but do not walk away. The negotiations enter a new phase — harder, slower, but more comprehensive.
You pocket the concessions and demand more, exploiting the moderates' desperation to show results. The talks collapse under the weight of your maximalism. The Omani intermediary goes home disgusted.

backchannel_ceasefire_test.scene: The ceasefire is seventy-two hours old. Both navies are standing down, tankers are beginning to move through the Strait, and the world is exhaling for the first time in weeks. Then your intelligence team flags anomalous activity: IRGC units repositioning near the coast, drone launches from Qeshm Island, encrypted traffic spiking on military frequencies. The ceasefire may be holding on the surface, but underneath, something is stirring.
consequences: You maintain the ceasefire and address the violations through diplomatic channels, trusting the framework to absorb the shock. Your ambassador in Muscat delivers a firm but private warning. The repositioning slows but does not stop.
You put your forces on heightened alert without breaking the ceasefire, matching Iranian repositioning with quiet movements of your own. The shadow dance continues beneath the surface of peace.

You declare the ceasefire violated and resume operations, arguing that Iran's actions prove the agreement was a ruse. The fragile peace shatters. The moderates in Tehran who staked their credibility on the deal are finished.

backchannel_counteroffer_response.scene: Iran's counteroffer arrives through the Omani channel. It is more modest than your proposal but more ambitious than their opening position. They will accept partial sanctions relief tied to a ninety-day de-escalation timeline, with verification by Omani observers. The deal is imperfect, full of loopholes and unresolved questions, but it is a deal. Your negotiators look at you expectantly.
consequences: You accept the counteroffer with minor modifications, choosing to build on imperfect progress rather than hold out for a perfect agreement that may never come. The signing is quiet, the handshakes tentative, the relief palpable.
You ask for more time to consult with allies, using the delay to shore up domestic support and international buy-in. The Iranians agree to wait, though their patience has limits.
You reject the counteroffer, demanding terms you know Iran cannot accept. The back channel goes silent. The Omani intermediary delivers your response and does not return.

hostage_situation_escalates.scene: The hostage situation has deteriorated. Iran has moved the detained tanker crews from the port facility to an unknown location inland. Your intelligence has lost track of them. The families are on television, pleading for action. Congressional pressure is mounting. Time is working against you — every day the hostages spend in Iranian custody makes their recovery more difficult and more dangerous.
consequences: You authorize sending a special envoy to Tehran, someone with enough stature to demand access to the hostages and enough credibility to negotiate their release. The envoy departs within hours, carrying your personal letter to the Iranian foreign minister.

You greenlight a rescue operation. SEAL Team Six has been training for this contingency since the crisis began. The plan is audacious — a night insertion by helicopter into hostile territory. The risks are astronomical, but so is the potential reward.

You increase diplomatic pressure through every available channel, mobilizing the Red Cross, the UN, and allied governments to demand humanitarian access. The multilateral approach is slower but builds an irresistible weight of international expectation.

hostage_envoy_result.scene: Your envoy has been in Tehran for three days. The negotiations have been grueling — marathon sessions in windowless rooms, accusations and counteraccusations, moments of progress followed by hours of deadlock. Now the envoy calls with an update: Iran is willing to release some hostages in exchange for unfreezing certain assets. Not all the hostages. Not all the assets. A partial deal in a world where partial is the best you can get.
consequences: You accept the partial release, calculating that getting some hostages home is worth the cost and that the precedent of negotiation will pave the way for the rest. The freed hostages arrive at Ramstein Air Base to cameras and tears.

You reject the partial deal and demand all hostages be released, threatening escalation if Iran continues to hold American-affiliated civilians. The envoy delivers the ultimatum with a steady voice and sweating palms.

hostage_rescue_result.scene: The rescue mission has launched. Black Hawks cross the Iranian coast at wave-top height, their rotors cutting the predawn darkness. The SEALs insert at the target compound under covering fire from AC-130 gunships. Then the operation goes sideways — the hostages have been moved, the compound is a trap, and your operators are taking fire from positions that were supposed to be empty. The extraction is chaotic, contested, and costly.
consequences: The rescue team fights its way out with casualties on both sides. No hostages are recovered. The mission is a failure by any measure — American servicemembers dead, hostages still captive, and Iran broadcasting footage of the abandoned equipment. You stare at the Situation Room screens and absorb the weight of what you have done.

mojtaba_power_play.scene: Your intelligence assets in Tehran report that Mojtaba Khamenei has made his move. He has purged three IRGC generals and installed loyalists in key positions. But the power play has left him isolated — he needs external validation to consolidate. Your station chief in the region sends a flash cable: Mojtaba's people want to talk. Not through intermediaries. Directly.
consequences: You open the direct channel, sending a trusted intelligence officer to a discreet meeting in a third country. The conversation is unprecedented — an American operative sitting across from the presumptive Supreme Leader's envoy, sketching the outlines of a new relationship on the back of a napkin.
You exploit Mojtaba's vulnerability, tightening sanctions and increasing military pressure while he is weak. The gamble is that a cornered Mojtaba will make concessions his father never would.

You share the intelligence about Mojtaba's overture with allies, building a multilateral approach to the succession while maintaining deniability about direct contact. The approach is cautious but risks losing the moment.

mojtaba_secret_deal.scene: The back channel with Mojtaba's faction has produced something extraordinary — a draft agreement that would fundamentally reshape Iran's relationship with the West. Sanctions relief, nuclear limits, Gulf security guarantees, proxy force drawdowns. It is the grand bargain that every administration since the Revolution has dreamed of and none has achieved. The catch: Mojtaba needs the deal to remain secret until he has fully consolidated power. One leak, and the hardliners will tear it apart — and possibly tear Mojtaba apart with it.
consequences: You sign the secret deal, committing American resources and credibility to a man you have never met face-to-face. The implementation begins quietly — sanctions waivers processed through back doors, military stand-downs disguised as routine rotations. The deception is elaborate, dangerous, and potentially historic.

You agree to a scaled-down version of the deal, reducing the scope and the secrecy requirement. The partial agreement is less transformative but more sustainable, requiring fewer lies and fewer risks.

You walk away, judging that a secret deal with an unproven leader is too fragile a foundation for lasting peace. You tell Mojtaba's envoy that America will negotiate publicly or not at all. The channel closes. The moment passes.

nuclear_inspection_crisis.scene: The IAEA inspection team has arrived at Fordow — and been denied access to the deep underground halls. The Iranian military has surrounded the facility with troops and declared the inspection 'suspended for security reasons.' Your inspectors are confined to a guest house while satellites show centrifuge halls operating at full capacity. The verification regime you staked your credibility on is collapsing in real time.
consequences: You authorize the strike on Fordow, ordering bunker-busting munitions to destroy what the inspectors cannot verify. The mountain shudders under the impact. The enrichment facility is buried deeper, this time under rubble. The world enters uncharted territory.

You present Iran with a Security Council ultimatum backed by your intelligence package. Forty-eight hours to grant full access, or face comprehensive sanctions and military consequences. The clock starts now. Iran's ambassador at the UN shifts uncomfortably in his chair.

You propose a face-saving compromise — a limited inspection of specific areas with a timeline for full access. The arrangement gives Iran a graceful retreat and gives you verification, even if imperfect. Your hawks call it capitulation. Your diplomats call it the only move that avoids war.

nuclear_ultimatum_response.scene: Iran's response to your nuclear ultimatum arrives at the stroke of the deadline. It is not a capitulation. It is not a rejection. It is a counter-ultimatum: withdraw your carrier groups from the Gulf within seventy-two hours, or Iran will expel all remaining international monitors and accelerate enrichment to weapons grade. The Supreme Leader appears on state television, flanked by generals, calling your bluff. The world has never been closer to a nuclear-armed Iran.
consequences: You call the bluff and launch strikes against Iran's nuclear infrastructure. Fordow, Natanz, Isfahan — the entire fuel cycle hit simultaneously in a single night of precision bombing. The mushroom clouds are conventional, but the strategic landscape has shifted irreversibly.
You blink. The carriers remain, but the ultimatum dies quietly in classified cables. Iran keeps its centrifuges. You keep your carriers. The status quo holds, but everyone knows who flinched.

nuclear_threshold.scene: The red phone rings at three in the morning. The CIA Director's voice is flat, drained of emotion, delivering the words that every president since 1979 has dreaded. Iran has crossed the nuclear threshold. Fordow's centrifuges have produced enough highly enriched uranium for a weapon. Weaponization timelines are uncertain — weeks, perhaps days. The Situation Room fills with people who have not slept, staring at satellite imagery of a mountain that now contains the end of the world as they know it.
consequences: You order an immediate strike on Fordow and all associated nuclear facilities. The B-2s are already airborne from Diego Garcia, carrying the thirty-thousand-pound Massive Ordnance Penetrators that were built for this exact scenario. You are about to bomb a nation's nuclear program. The last president who considered this chose not to.

You authorize a broader military campaign — not just the nuclear sites but Iran's military infrastructure, command and control, air defenses. If you are going to war, you reason, do it once and do it completely. The generals nod. They have been planning this for decades.

You take the intelligence to the UN Security Council, demanding emergency sanctions and international action. The diplomatic route feels inadequate against the ticking clock, but you refuse to be the president who started a war on the strength of intelligence that might be wrong.

You open an emergency back channel to Tehran, offering a comprehensive deal: sanctions relief, security guarantees, diplomatic recognition — everything on the table — in exchange for verifiable dismantlement. It is the most generous offer any American president has ever made to Iran, and it tastes like surrender.

You do nothing visible. You increase surveillance, position assets, prepare contingencies, and wait for Iran to make its next move. The strategic patience is agonizing, but you refuse to let Tehran's timeline dictate your actions.

three_seizures.scene: Three tankers seized in a single morning. The IRGC has executed a coordinated operation of terrifying precision — fast boats converging simultaneously on vessels in three different sections of the Strait, boarding parties storming bridges before escort warships can respond. The Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed. Global oil supply has just lost a fifth of its volume. Markets are in freefall. The President of the United States is being woken for the third time tonight.
consequences: You order an immediate military response — strike the IRGC fast-boat bases, destroy their command and control, cripple their ability to conduct further operations. The strikes begin within the hour. American firepower meets Iranian infrastructure in a one-sided exchange that reshapes the coastline.

You impose crippling economic sanctions and a full naval blockade of Iranian ports, strangling their oil exports as they strangle yours. The economic warfare is total and reciprocal. Both economies begin to bleed.

You go to the UN, armed with satellite footage and intercepted communications, demanding international action. The Security Council chamber becomes a courtroom. China and Russia shift in their seats, calculating their interests.

You order a full carrier strike on every IRGC naval facility along the Iranian coast. The operation is massive — the largest American military action in the Gulf since 2003. When it is over, Iran's ability to threaten shipping has been permanently degraded. So has any remaining chance of a diplomatic resolution.

friendly_fire.scene: The worst has happened. In the fog of a nighttime naval engagement, an American destroyer has fired on and struck a vessel that turns out to be a Bahraini coast guard cutter operating in the same waters. Casualties are significant. The Bahraini government is in shock — they are your ally, their base hosts your fleet, and your weapons have just killed their sailors. The incident is already on social media, uploaded by a fishing boat that witnessed the explosion.
consequences: You immediately acknowledge the incident and take full responsibility, ordering a stand-down of naval operations pending investigation. The President calls the Bahraini king personally. The apology is abject and sincere. The damage to the alliance will take years to repair.

You express condolences but frame the incident as an inevitable consequence of operating in a high-threat environment, shifting some blame to the fog of war and inadequate coordination. The Bahraini government sees through the deflection immediately.

You attempt to suppress the details, classifying the investigation and pressuring social media platforms to remove the footage. The cover-up fails within twenty-four hours, compounding the original tragedy with a crisis of credibility.

You propose joint rules of engagement with all allied navies in the theater, turning the tragedy into a catalyst for better coordination. The initiative cannot undo the dead, but it might prevent the next friendly-fire incident.

cascade_crisis.scene: Everything is happening at once. Houthi missiles are streaking toward Red Sea shipping. Hezbollah rockets are hitting targets in Iraq. An IRGC fast-boat swarm is converging on a tanker convoy in the Strait. Your forces are engaged on three fronts simultaneously, and the situation board looks like a war that has already started without anyone formally declaring it. Your theater commander is requesting reinforcements he knows you do not have. The cascade has begun.
consequences: You prioritize the Strait, ordering all available assets to protect the tanker convoy while accepting risk on the other fronts. The choice is agonizing — you are abandoning allies and bases to focus on the economic chokepoint. Houthi missiles and Hezbollah rockets will fall unanswered.

You escalate to a full regional war footing, authorizing strikes against all three threat axes simultaneously. The American military machine shifts into high gear — every carrier, every bomber, every special operations unit in the region receives a target package. This is the war nobody wanted.

You reach for the diplomatic toolkit, proposing an emergency ceasefire through the UN while your forces play defense. The diplomatic gambit is a long shot, but the alternative is a regional conflagration that could burn for years.

You address the nation, acknowledging the severity of the crisis and calling for calm. Behind the scenes, you authorize only defensive operations, refusing to be the one who escalates a bad situation into an irreversible catastrophe.

carrier_hit.scene: The explosion rocks the USS Eisenhower amidships, a plume of fire and black smoke erupting from the flight deck. An Iranian anti-ship ballistic missile has struck an American aircraft carrier — the first time a US carrier has been hit by enemy action since World War Two. Damage control teams are fighting fires while the ship lists to starboard. Casualty reports are streaming in. The carrier is still afloat, still fighting, but the symbol of American naval invincibility has just been shattered on live television.
consequences: You order an immediate and overwhelming retaliatory strike against every military target of value in Iran. The response is biblical in its scope — airbases, naval installations, missile batteries, command centers, all hit within a single coordinated wave. When the sun rises, Iran's conventional military capability has been reduced by half.

You authorize a proportional response — targeted strikes against the specific units and facilities responsible for the attack. The restraint in the face of such provocation is either strategic wisdom or unforgivable weakness, depending on who you ask.

You order the Eisenhower evacuated and towed to safety, focusing entirely on saving the ship and her crew before making any retaliatory decisions. The world watches an American carrier limping away from the fight. Some call it prudence. Others call it retreat.

You go on national television, show the footage of the burning carrier, and rally the nation for what comes next. The speech is measured, resolute, and devastating. You announce that America will respond at a time and place of its choosing. Iran begins to wonder when and where that will be.

crisis: The lights in the Situation Room shift to red.
An aide bursts through the door, a classified folder clutched to his chest.
The klaxon sounds twice — incoming crisis.
Every phone in the room begins ringing simultaneously.
The watch officer's voice cuts through the background noise: 'Flash traffic, sir.'
military: The war room screens flicker with new tactical data.
Satellite imagery refreshes on the main display, and the room goes quiet.
Your military liaison steps forward with a targeting folder.
The sound of jet engines on the carrier feed fills the room.
A Navy captain appears on the secure video link, his face lit by red battle lamps.
diplomatic: The secure phone rings on the mahogany desk.
Your aide slides a diplomatic cable across the table, marked IMMEDIATE.
The ambassador is waiting in the anteroom, his expression carefully neutral.
A translated message arrives from the Omani intermediary.
The Secretary of State appears at the door with news that changes the calculus.
economic: The Bloomberg terminal in the corner flashes red.
Markets are moving — you can see it in the Treasury liaison's face.
Your economic advisor sets down a chart with numbers that make your stomach drop.
The price ticker on the wall clicks upward with mechanical inevitability.
An urgent call from the Fed Chairman is holding on line two.
intel: An analyst from the watch floor appears at your door, a red-bordered folder in hand.
A red-bordered folder lands on your desk with the weight of consequence.
The CIA briefer speaks in the flat tones reserved for unwelcome certainties.
Your intelligence liaison pulls you aside after the morning brief.
A signal intercept, translated overnight, changes everything you thought you knew.
default: The room falls quiet as a new development arrives.
Your senior advisor approaches with the expression that means trouble.
The morning routine breaks when a staffer appears with an urgent update.
A notification appears on the classified terminal, priority flagged.
The background hum of the Situation Room shifts in pitch — something has changed.

timedUrgency: The clock is ticking. They need your answer now.
Every second of delay costs you leverage.
Your staff is waiting, pens hovering over the action memo.
The Admiral on the other end of the line clears his throat impatiently.
The tactical display updates in real time. The window is closing.
Your watch officer glances at the clock. The situation is deteriorating.
The red phone is blinking. Someone on the other end is counting the seconds.
Time is a luxury you no longer have.
